Though not declared official *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")*, the Spanish language is the only one used in the wording of laws, decrees, resolutions, official documents and public acts thus making it the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* official language. | |

**Argentina**,[[B]](#cite_note-15) officially the **Argentine Republic**,[[C]](#cite_note-17)[[D]](#cite_note-18) is a country located in the [southern cone](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone") of [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") and with a [claimed portion](/wiki/Antarctic_Treaty_System "Antarctic Treaty System") of [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"). It covers an area of 2,780,085 km2 (1,073,397 mi2),[[A]](#cite_note-excl_area-9) making it the [second-largest country in South America](/wiki/List_of_South_American_countries_by_area "List of South American countries by area") after [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), the fourth-largest country in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), and the [eighth-largest country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area") in the world. Argentina shares the bulk of the [Southern Cone](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone") with [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") to the west, and is also bordered by [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia") and [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay") to the north, Brazil to the northeast, [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") and the South [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to the east, and the [Drake Passage](/wiki/Drake_Passage "Drake Passage") to the south. Argentina is a [federal state](/wiki/Federation "Federation") subdivided into twenty-three [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Argentina "Provinces of Argentina"), and one [autonomous city](/wiki/Autonomous_city "Autonomous city"), which is the [federal capital](/wiki/Federal_capital "Federal capital") and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Argentina_by_population "List of cities in Argentina by population") of the nation, [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"). The provinces and the capital have their own constitutions, but exist under a [federal system](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism"). Argentina claims sovereignty over the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands_sovereignty_dispute "Falkland Islands sovereignty dispute"), [South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands](/wiki/South_Georgia_and_the_South_Sandwich_Islands_sovereignty_dispute "South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands sovereignty dispute"), the [Southern Patagonian Ice Field](/wiki/Southern_Patagonian_Ice_Field_dispute "Southern Patagonian Ice Field dispute"), and [a part of Antarctica](/wiki/Argentine_Antarctica "Argentine Antarctica").

The earliest recorded human presence in modern-day Argentina dates back to the [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") period.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbad_de_Santillán197117-19)[[15]](#cite_note-20) The [Inca Empire](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire") expanded to the northwest of the country in pre-Columbian times. The modern country has its roots in [Spanish colonisation](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") of the region during the 16th century.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrow1992128-21) Argentina rose as the successor state of the [Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata"),[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevene194811Sánchez_Viamonte1948196–97Vanossi196411-22) a Spanish [overseas viceroyalty](/wiki/Viceroyalty "Viceroyalty") founded in 1776. The [Argentine Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Argentine_Declaration_of_Independence "Argentine Declaration of Independence") on July 9 of 1816 and the [Argentine War of Independence](/wiki/Argentine_War_of_Independence "Argentine War of Independence") (1810–1825) were followed by an [extended civil war](/wiki/Argentine_Civil_Wars "Argentine Civil Wars") that lasted until 1880, culminating in the country's reorganisation as a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation"). The country thereafter enjoyed relative peace and stability, with [several subsequent waves of European immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_Argentina "Immigration to Argentina"), mainly of [Italians](/wiki/Italian_Argentines "Italian Argentines") and [Spaniards](/wiki/Spanish_Argentines "Spanish Argentines"), influencing its [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Argentina "Culture of Argentina") and [demography](/wiki/Demographics_of_Argentina "Demographics of Argentina").[[18]](#cite_note-23)[[19]](#cite_note-LaMatanza-24)[[20]](#cite_note-25)[[21]](#cite_note-26)

The [National Autonomist Party](/wiki/National_Autonomist_Party "National Autonomist Party") dominated national politics in the period called the [Conservative Republic](/wiki/Generation_of_%2780 "Generation of '80"), from 1880 until the [1916 elections](/wiki/1916_Argentine_general_election "1916 Argentine general election"). The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") led to the [first coup d'état in 1930](/wiki/1930_Argentine_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1930 Argentine coup d'état") led by [José Félix Uriburu](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_F%C3%A9lix_Uriburu "José Félix Uriburu"), beginning the so-called "[Infamous Decade](/wiki/Infamous_Decade "Infamous Decade")" (1930–1943). After that coup, four more followed in [1943](/wiki/1943_Argentine_Revolution "1943 Argentine Revolution"), [1955](/wiki/Revoluci%C3%B3n_Libertadora "Revolución Libertadora"), [1962](/wiki/1962_Argentine_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1962 Argentine coup d'état"), and [1966](/wiki/Argentine_Revolution "Argentine Revolution"). Following the death of President [Juan Perón](/wiki/Juan_Per%C3%B3n "Juan Perón") in 1974, his widow and vice president, [Isabel Perón](/wiki/Isabel_Per%C3%B3n "Isabel Perón"), ascended to the presidency, before being overthrown in the final coup [in 1976](/wiki/1976_Argentine_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1976 Argentine coup d'état"). The following [military junta](/wiki/National_Reorganization_Process "National Reorganization Process") persecuted and murdered thousands of political critics, activists, and leftists in the [Dirty War](/wiki/Dirty_War "Dirty War"), a period of [state terrorism](/wiki/State_terrorism "State terrorism") and civil unrest that lasted until the election of [Raúl Alfonsín](/wiki/Ra%C3%BAl_Alfons%C3%ADn "Raúl Alfonsín") as president in [1983](/wiki/1983_Argentine_general_election "1983 Argentine general election").

Argentina is a [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power"), and retains its historic status as a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") in international affairs.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood198818Solomon19973-27)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuntington20006Nierop200161Lake200955Papadopoulos2010283Malamud20119Boughton2012101-28)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris198863AdlerGreve200978Ruiz-DanaGoldschagClaroBlanco200918-29) A [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"),[[25]](#cite_note-Major_Non-NATO_Ally_Status-30) Argentina is a [developing country](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") with the second-highest [HDI](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (human development index) in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") after [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile").[[26]](#cite_note-31) It maintains the [second-largest economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Argentina "Economy of Argentina") in South America, and is a member of [G-15](/wiki/Group_of_15 "Group of 15") and [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20"). Argentina is also a founding member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group"), [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), [Mercosur](/wiki/Mercosur "Mercosur"), [Community of Latin American and Caribbean States](/wiki/Community_of_Latin_American_and_Caribbean_States "Community of Latin American and Caribbean States") and the [Organization of Ibero-American States](/wiki/Organization_of_Ibero-American_States "Organization of Ibero-American States").

## Etymology

Main article: [Etymology of Argentina](/wiki/Etymology_of_Argentina "Etymology of Argentina")

The description of the region by the word *Argentina* has been found on a [Venetian](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") map in 1536.[[27]](#cite_note-32)

In English, the name *Argentina* comes from the [Spanish language](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"); however, the naming itself is not Spanish, but [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"). *Argentina* ([masculine](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") *argentino*) means in Italian '(made) of silver, silver coloured', derived from the Latin *argentum* for silver. In Italian, the adjective or the [proper noun](/wiki/Proper_noun "Proper noun") is often used in an autonomous way as a substantive and replaces it and it is said *l'Argentina*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The name *Argentina* was probably first given by the Venetian and Genoese navigators, such as [Giovanni Caboto](/wiki/Giovanni_Caboto "Giovanni Caboto"). In Spanish and Portuguese, the words for 'silver' are respectively *plata* and *prata* and '(made) of silver' is *plateado* and *prateado*, although *argento* for 'silver' and *argentado* for 'covered in silver' exist in Spanish. *Argentina* was first associated with the [silver mountains legend](/wiki/Sierra_de_la_Plata "Sierra de la Plata"), widespread among the first European explorers of the [La Plata Basin](/wiki/La_Plata_Basin "La Plata Basin").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERock19876,_8Edwards20087-33)

The first written use of the name in Spanish can be traced to *[La Argentina](/wiki/La_Argentina_(poem) "La Argentina (poem)")*,[[E]](#cite_note-34) a 1602 poem by [Martín del Barco Centenera](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_del_Barco_Centenera "Martín del Barco Centenera") describing the region.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETraba198515,_71-35) Although "Argentina" was already in common usage by the 18th century, the country was formally named "[Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata")" by the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), and "[United Provinces of the Río de la Plata](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "United Provinces of the Río de la Plata")" after independence.

The [1826 constitution](/wiki/Argentine_Constitution_of_1826 "Argentine Constitution of 1826") included the first use of the name "Argentine Republic" in legal documents.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentina1826,_art._1-36) The name "Argentine Confederation" was also commonly used and was formalised in the [Argentine Constitution of 1853](/wiki/Argentine_Constitution_of_1853 "Argentine Constitution of 1853").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentina1853,_Preamble-37) In 1860 a presidential decree settled the country's name as "Argentine Republic",[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosenblat196478-38) and that year's constitutional amendment ruled all the names since 1810 as legally valid.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentina1860_amd.,_art._35-39)[[F]](#cite_note-40)

In [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), the country was traditionally called "the Argentine", mimicking the typical [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") usage *la Argentina*[[34]](#cite_note-41) and perhaps resulting from a mistaken shortening of the longer name "Argentine Republic". "The Argentine" fell out of fashion during the mid-to-late 20th century, and now the country is referred to as "Argentina".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

Main article: [History of Argentina](/wiki/History_of_Argentina "History of Argentina")

### Pre-Columbian era

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Argentina](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Argentina "Indigenous peoples in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Cueva_de_las_Manos_%286811931046%29.jpg/250px-Cueva_de_las_Manos_%286811931046%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cueva_de_las_Manos_(6811931046).jpg)

The [Cave of the Hands](/wiki/Cueva_de_las_Manos "Cueva de las Manos") in [Santa Cruz province](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_province,_Argentina "Santa Cruz province, Argentina")

Evidence suggests that humans inhabited parts of what is now Argentina as early as 21,000 years ago. In 2015, fossilised bones of a large, extinct armored mammal called *[Neosclerocalyptus](/wiki/Neosclerocalyptus "Neosclerocalyptus")* were unearthed near [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"). These bones bore cut marks indicative of butchering with stone tools, implying human activity during the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum").[[35]](#cite_note-42)[[36]](#cite_note-43) Further south, the [Piedra Museo](/wiki/Piedra_Museo "Piedra Museo") site in [Santa Cruz Province](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_Province,_Argentina "Santa Cruz Province, Argentina") has yielded human remains and artifacts dating back approximately 11,000 years. Discoveries at this site include spearheads associated with extinct megafauna such as *[Mylodon](/wiki/Mylodon "Mylodon")* and *[Hippidion](/wiki/Hippidion "Hippidion")*, highlighting the advanced hunting practices of early inhabitants.[[37]](#cite_note-44)[[38]](#cite_note-:0-45) Another significant site is the [Cueva de las Manos](/wiki/Cueva_de_las_Manos "Cueva de las Manos") ([Cave of the Hands](/wiki/Cueva_de_las_Manos "Cueva de las Manos")), also located in [Santa Cruz](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_Province,_Argentina "Santa Cruz Province, Argentina"). This cave features stenciled handprints and hunting scenes created between 7,300 BC and 700 AD, offering insights into the lives of early hunter-gatherer communities.[[39]](#cite_note-46)[[38]](#cite_note-:0-45)

Until the period of European colonisation, Argentina was relatively sparsely populated by a wide number of diverse cultures with different social organisations,[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards200812-47) which can be divided into three main groups.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbad_de_Santillán197118–19-48) The first group were basic hunters and food gatherers without the development of [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery"), such as the [Selkʼnam](/wiki/Selk%CA%BCnam_people "Selkʼnam people") and [Yaghan](/wiki/Yaghan_people "Yaghan people") in the extreme south. The second group were advanced hunters and food gatherers, who included the [Puelche](/wiki/Puelche_people "Puelche people"), [Querandí](/wiki/Querand%C3%AD "Querandí") and Serranos in the centre-east; and the [Tehuelche](/wiki/Tehuelche_people "Tehuelche people") in the south, all of them conquered by the [Mapuche](/wiki/Mapuche "Mapuche") spreading from [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"),[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards200813-49) and the [Kom](/wiki/Kom_people_(South_America) "Kom people (South America)") and [Wichi](/wiki/Wichi "Wichi") in the north. The last group were farmers with pottery, such as the [Charrúa](/wiki/Charr%C3%BAa "Charrúa"), [Minuane](/wiki/Minuane "Minuane") and [Guaraní](/wiki/Guaran%C3%AD_people "Guaraní people") in the northeast, with [slash and burn](/wiki/Slash_and_burn_agriculture "Slash and burn agriculture") semisedentary existence;[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards200812-47) the advanced [Diaguita](/wiki/Diaguita "Diaguita") sedentary [trading culture](/wiki/Trade "Trade") in the northwest, which was conquered by the [Inca Empire](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire") around 1480; the [Tonocoté](/wiki/Tonocot%C3%A9 "Tonocoté") and [Hênîa and Kâmîare](/wiki/Comechingones "Comechingones") in the country's centre, and the [Huarpe](/wiki/Huarpe "Huarpe") in the centre-west, a culture that raised [llama](/wiki/Llama "Llama") cattle and was strongly influenced by the Incas.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards200812-47)

### Colonial era

Main article: [Colonial Argentina](/wiki/Colonial_Argentina "Colonial Argentina")

See also: [Spanish colonization of the Americas](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas")

[![Painting showing the surrender during the British invasions of the Río de la Plata.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/74/La_Reconquista_de_Buenos_Aires.jpg/250px-La_Reconquista_de_Buenos_Aires.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Reconquista_de_Buenos_Aires.jpg)

The surrender of Beresford to [Santiago de Liniers](/wiki/Santiago_de_Liniers "Santiago de Liniers") during the [British invasions of the Río de la Plata](/wiki/British_invasions_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "British invasions of the Río de la Plata")

Europeans first arrived in the region with the 1502 voyage of [Amerigo Vespucci](/wiki/Amerigo_Vespucci_(explorer) "Amerigo Vespucci (explorer)"). The Spanish navigators [Juan Díaz de Solís](/wiki/Juan_D%C3%ADaz_de_Sol%C3%ADs "Juan Díaz de Solís") and [Sebastian Cabot](/wiki/Sebastian_Cabot_(explorer) "Sebastian Cabot (explorer)") visited the territory that is now Argentina in 1516 and 1526, respectively.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrow1992128-21) In 1536 [Pedro de Mendoza](/wiki/Pedro_de_Mendoza "Pedro de Mendoza") founded the small settlement of [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"), which was abandoned in 1541.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrow1992129–32-50)

Further colonisation efforts came from [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay")—establishing the [Governorate of the Río de la Plata](/wiki/Governorate_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Governorate of the Río de la Plata")—[Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") and Chile.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbad_de_Santillán197196–140-51) [Francisco de Aguirre](/wiki/Francisco_de_Aguirre_(conquistador) "Francisco de Aguirre (conquistador)") founded [Santiago del Estero](/wiki/Santiago_del_Estero "Santiago del Estero") in 1553. [Londres](/wiki/Londres,_Catamarca "Londres, Catamarca") was founded in 1558; [Mendoza](/wiki/Mendoza,_Argentina "Mendoza, Argentina"), in 1561; [San Juan](/wiki/San_Juan,_Argentina "San Juan, Argentina"), in 1562; [San Miguel de Tucumán](/wiki/San_Miguel_de_Tucum%C3%A1n "San Miguel de Tucumán"), in 1565.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrow1992353-52) [Juan de Garay](/wiki/Juan_de_Garay "Juan de Garay") founded [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_Argentina "Santa Fe, Argentina") in 1573 and the same year [Jerónimo Luis de Cabrera](/wiki/Jer%C3%B3nimo_Luis_de_Cabrera "Jerónimo Luis de Cabrera") set up [Córdoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Argentina "Córdoba, Argentina").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrow1992134-53) Garay went further south to re-found Buenos Aires in 1580.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrow1992135-54) [San Luis](/wiki/San_Luis,_Argentina "San Luis, Argentina") was established in 1596.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrow1992353-52)

The [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") subordinated the economic potential of the Argentine territory to the immediate wealth of the silver and gold mines in [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia") and Peru, and as such it became part of the [Viceroyalty of Peru](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_Peru "Viceroyalty of Peru") until the creation of the [Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata") in 1776 with Buenos Aires as its capital.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrow1992347-55)

Buenos Aires repelled [two ill-fated British invasions](/wiki/British_invasions_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "British invasions of the Río de la Plata") in 1806 and 1807.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrow1992421-56) The ideas of the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") and the example of the first [Atlantic Revolutions](/wiki/Atlantic_Revolutions "Atlantic Revolutions") generated criticism of the [absolutist monarchy](/wiki/Absolutist_monarchy "Absolutist monarchy") that ruled the country. As in the rest of Spanish America, the overthrow of [Ferdinand VII](/wiki/Ferdinand_VII_of_Spain "Ferdinand VII of Spain") during the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") created great concern.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbad_de_Santillán1971194ff-57)

### Independence and civil wars

Main articles: [Argentine War of Independence](/wiki/Argentine_War_of_Independence "Argentine War of Independence") and [Argentine Civil Wars](/wiki/Argentine_Civil_Wars "Argentine Civil Wars")

[![Painting of San Martín holding the Argentine flag](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Jos%C3%A9_de_San_Mart%C3%ADn_%28retrato%2C_c.1828%29.jpg/250px-Jos%C3%A9_de_San_Mart%C3%ADn_%28retrato%2C_c.1828%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jos%C3%A9_de_San_Mart%C3%ADn_(retrato,_c.1828).jpg)

General [José de San Martín](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_San_Mart%C3%ADn "José de San Martín"), [*Libertador*](/wiki/Libertadores "Libertadores") of Argentina, [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") and [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru")[[51]](#cite_note-58)

Beginning a process from which Argentina was to emerge as successor state to the Viceroyalty,[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevene194811Sánchez_Viamonte1948196–97Vanossi196411-22) the 1810 [May Revolution](/wiki/May_Revolution "May Revolution") replaced the viceroy [Baltasar Hidalgo de Cisneros](/wiki/Baltasar_Hidalgo_de_Cisneros "Baltasar Hidalgo de Cisneros") with the [First Junta](/wiki/Primera_Junta "Primera Junta"), a new government in [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires") made up from locals.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbad_de_Santillán1971194ff-57) In the first clashes of the Independence War the Junta crushed a royalist [counter-revolution in Córdoba](/wiki/Liniers_Counter-revolution "Liniers Counter-revolution"),[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERock198781-59) but failed to overcome those of the [Banda Oriental](/wiki/Banda_Oriental "Banda Oriental"), [Upper Peru](/wiki/First_Upper_Peru_campaign "First Upper Peru campaign") and [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_campaign "Paraguay campaign"), which later became independent states.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERock198782–83-60) The French-Argentine [Hippolyte Bouchard](/wiki/Hippolyte_Bouchard "Hippolyte Bouchard") then brought his fleet to wage war against Spain overseas and attacked [Spanish California](/wiki/Spanish_California "Spanish California"), [Spanish Peru](/wiki/Spanish_Peru "Spanish Peru") and [Spanish Philippines](/wiki/Spanish_Philippines "Spanish Philippines"). He secured the allegiance of escaped Filipinos in San Blas who defected from the Spanish to join the Argentine navy, due to common Argentine and Philippine grievances against Spanish colonisation.[[54]](#cite_note-61)[[55]](#cite_note-62) Jose de San Martin's brother, [Juan Fermín de San Martín](/wiki/Juan_Ferm%C3%ADn_de_San_Mart%C3%ADn "Juan Fermín de San Martín"), was already in the Philippines and drumming up revolutionary fervor prior to this.[[56]](#cite_note-ins-63) At a later date, the Argentine sign of Inca origin, the [Sun of May](/wiki/Sun_of_May "Sun of May") was adopted as a symbol by the Filipinos in the [Philippine Revolution](/wiki/Republic_of_Biak-na-Bato "Republic of Biak-na-Bato") against Spain. He also secured the diplomatic recognition of Argentina from King [Kamehameha I](/wiki/Kamehameha_I "Kamehameha I") of the [Kingdom of Hawaii](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hawaii "Kingdom of Hawaii"). Historian Pacho O'Donnell affirms that Hawaii was the first state that recognised Argentina's independence.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Donnell1998-64) He was finally arrested in 1819 by Chilean patriots.

Revolutionaries split into two antagonist groups: the [Centralists](/wiki/Unitarian_Party "Unitarian Party") and the [Federalists](/wiki/Federales_(Argentina) "Federales (Argentina)")—a move that would define Argentina's first decades of independence.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis200339–40-65) The [Assembly of the Year XIII](/wiki/Assembly_of_the_Year_XIII "Assembly of the Year XIII") appointed [Gervasio Antonio de Posadas](/wiki/Gervasio_Antonio_de_Posadas "Gervasio Antonio de Posadas") as Argentina's first [Supreme Director](/wiki/Supreme_Director_of_the_United_Provinces_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Supreme Director of the United Provinces of the Río de la Plata").[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis200339–40-65)

On 9 July 1816, the [Congress of Tucumán](/wiki/Congress_of_Tucum%C3%A1n "Congress of Tucumán") formalised the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Argentine_Declaration_of_Independence "Argentine Declaration of Independence"),[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERock198792Lewis200341-66) which is now celebrated as Independence Day, a national holiday.[[60]](#cite_note-67) One year later General [Martín Miguel de Güemes](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_Miguel_de_G%C3%BCemes "Martín Miguel de Güemes") stopped royalists on the north. General [José de San Martín](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_San_Mart%C3%ADn "José de San Martín") joined [Bernardo O'Higgins](/wiki/Bernardo_O%27Higgins "Bernardo O'Higgins"), and they led a combined army [across the Andes](/wiki/Crossing_of_the_Andes "Crossing of the Andes") and secured the independence of Chile; then it was sent by O'Higgins orders to the Spanish stronghold of [Lima](/wiki/Lima "Lima") and proclaimed the [independence of Peru](/wiki/Independence_of_Peru "Independence of Peru").[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011349–53vol._I-68)[[G]](#cite_note-70) In 1819 Buenos Aires enacted a [centralist constitution](/wiki/Argentine_Constitution_of_1819 "Argentine Constitution of 1819") that was soon [abrogated](/wiki/Repeal "Repeal") by federalists.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis200341-71)

Some of the most important figures of Argentine independence made a proposal known as the [Inca plan](/wiki/Inca_plan "Inca plan") of 1816, which proposed that the [United Provinces of the Río de la Plata](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "United Provinces of the Río de la Plata") (Present Argentina) should be a monarchy, led by a descendant of the [Inca](/wiki/Sapa_Inca "Sapa Inca"). Juan Bautista Túpac Amaru (half-brother of [Túpac Amaru II](/wiki/T%C3%BApac_Amaru_II "Túpac Amaru II")) was proposed as monarch.[[64]](#cite_note-72) Some examples of those who supported this proposal were [Manuel Belgrano](/wiki/Manuel_Belgrano "Manuel Belgrano"), [José de San Martín](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_San_Mart%C3%ADn "José de San Martín") and [Martín Miguel de Güemes](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_Miguel_de_G%C3%BCemes "Martín Miguel de Güemes"). The [Congress of Tucumán](/wiki/Congress_of_Tucum%C3%A1n "Congress of Tucumán") finally decided to reject the Inca plan, creating instead a republican, centralist state.[[65]](#cite_note-73)[[66]](#cite_note-74)

The 1820 [Battle of Cepeda](/wiki/Battle_of_Cepeda_(1820) "Battle of Cepeda (1820)"), fought between the Centralists and the Federalists, resulted in the *end of the Supreme Director rule*. In 1826 Buenos Aires enacted another [centralist constitution](/wiki/Argentine_Constitution_of_1826 "Argentine Constitution of 1826"), with [Bernardino Rivadavia](/wiki/Bernardino_Rivadavia "Bernardino Rivadavia") being appointed as the first president of the country. However, the interior provinces soon rose against him, forced his resignation and discarded the constitution.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis200343-75) Centralists and Federalists resumed the civil war; the latter prevailed and formed the [Argentine Confederation](/wiki/Argentine_Confederation "Argentine Confederation") in 1831, led by [Juan Manuel de Rosas](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_de_Rosas "Juan Manuel de Rosas").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis200345-76) During his regime he faced a [French blockade](/wiki/French_blockade_to_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "French blockade to the Río de la Plata") (1838–1840), the [War of the Confederation](/wiki/War_of_the_Confederation "War of the Confederation") (1836–1839), and an [Anglo-French blockade](/wiki/Anglo-French_blockade_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Anglo-French blockade of the Río de la Plata") (1845–1850), but remained undefeated and prevented further loss of national territory.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis200346–47-77) His trade restriction policies, however, angered the interior provinces and in 1852 [Justo José de Urquiza](/wiki/Justo_Jos%C3%A9_de_Urquiza "Justo José de Urquiza"), another powerful [caudillo](/wiki/Caudillo "Caudillo"), [beat him out of power](/wiki/Battle_of_Caseros "Battle of Caseros"). As the new president of the Confederation, Urquiza enacted the [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") and federal 1853 Constitution. [Buenos Aires seceded](/wiki/State_of_Buenos_Aires "State of Buenos Aires") but was forced back into the Confederation after being defeated in the 1859 [Battle of Cepeda](/wiki/Battle_of_Cepeda_(1859) "Battle of Cepeda (1859)").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis200348–50-78)

### Rise of the modern nation

Main articles: [List of Presidents of Argentina](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_Argentina "List of Presidents of Argentina"), [Generation of '80](/wiki/Generation_of_%2780 "Generation of '80"), and [Infamous Decade](/wiki/Infamous_Decade "Infamous Decade")

See also: [Argentine–Chilean naval arms race](/wiki/Argentine%E2%80%93Chilean_naval_arms_race "Argentine–Chilean naval arms race") and [South American dreadnought race](/wiki/South_American_dreadnought_race "South American dreadnought race")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/25_de_mayo_por_F._Fortuny.jpg/250px-25_de_mayo_por_F._Fortuny.jpg)](/wiki/File:25_de_mayo_por_F._Fortuny.jpg)

People gathered in front of the [Buenos Aires Cabildo](/wiki/Buenos_Aires_Cabildo "Buenos Aires Cabildo") during the [May Revolution](/wiki/May_Revolution "May Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Italian_immigrants_buenos_aires.jpg/250px-Italian_immigrants_buenos_aires.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italian_immigrants_buenos_aires.jpg)

Immigrants [from Italy](/wiki/Italian_Argentines "Italian Argentines") arriving in Buenos Aires, during the [great European immigration wave to Argentina](/wiki/Great_European_immigration_wave_to_Argentina "Great European immigration wave to Argentina")

Overpowering Urquiza in the 1861 [Battle of Pavón](/wiki/Battle_of_Pav%C3%B3n "Battle of Pavón"), [Bartolomé Mitre](/wiki/Bartolom%C3%A9_Mitre "Bartolomé Mitre") secured Buenos Aires' predominance and was elected as the first president of the reunified country. He was followed by [Domingo Faustino Sarmiento](/wiki/Domingo_Faustino_Sarmiento "Domingo Faustino Sarmiento") and [Nicolás Avellaneda](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Avellaneda "Nicolás Avellaneda"); these three presidencies set up the basis of the modern Argentine State.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011363–541vol._I-79)

Starting with [Julio Argentino Roca](/wiki/Julio_Argentino_Roca "Julio Argentino Roca") in 1880, ten consecutive federal governments emphasised [liberal economic policies](/wiki/Economic_liberalism "Economic liberalism"). The [massive wave of European immigration](/wiki/Immigration_in_Argentina "Immigration in Argentina") they promoted—second only to the United States'—led to a near-reinvention of Argentine society and economy that by 1908 had placed the country as the seventh wealthiest[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoltVan_Zanden2013-80) developed nation[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDíaz_Alejandro19701-81) in the world. Driven by this [immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") wave and decreasing mortality, the Argentine population grew fivefold and the economy 15-fold:[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis199018–30-82) from 1870 to 1910, Argentina's [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") exports went from 100,000 to 2,500,000 t (110,000 to 2,760,000 short tons) per year, while frozen beef exports increased from 25,000 to 365,000 t (28,000 to 402,000 short tons) per year,[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMosk199088–89-83) placing Argentina as one of the world's top five exporters.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECruz199010-84) Its railway mileage rose from 503 to 31,104 km (313 to 19,327 mi).[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDíaz_Alejandro19702–3-85) Fostered by a new [public, compulsory, free and secular education](/wiki/Argentine_Law_1420 "Argentine Law 1420") system, [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") quickly increased from 22% to 65%, a level higher than most [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") nations would reach even fifty years later.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECruz199010-84) Furthermore, real [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") grew so fast that despite the huge immigration influx, [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") between 1862 and 1920 went from 67% of developed country levels to 100%:[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDíaz_Alejandro19702–3-85) In 1865, Argentina was already one of the top 25 nations by per capita income. By 1908, it had surpassed Denmark, Canada and the Netherlands to reach 7th place—behind Switzerland, New Zealand, Australia, the United States, the United Kingdom and Belgium. Argentina's per capita income was 70% higher than Italy's, 90% higher than Spain's, 180% higher than Japan's and 400% higher than [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")'s.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoltVan_Zanden2013-80) Despite these unique achievements, the country was slow to meet its original goals of industrialisation:[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011567–625vol._I-86) after the steep development of capital-intensive local industries in the 1920s, a significant part of the manufacturing sector remained labour-intensive in the 1930s.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis199037–38-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/La_conquista_del_desierto.jpg/250px-La_conquista_del_desierto.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_conquista_del_desierto.jpg)

*[Conquest of the Desert](/wiki/Conquest_of_the_Desert "Conquest of the Desert")*, by [Juan Manuel Blanes](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_Blanes "Juan Manuel Blanes") *(fragment showing [Julio Argentino Roca](/wiki/Julio_Argentino_Roca "Julio Argentino Roca"), at the front, a major figure of the [Generation of '80](/wiki/Generation_of_%2780 "Generation of '80"))*[[80]](#cite_note-88)

Between 1878 and 1884, the so-called [Conquest of the Desert](/wiki/Conquest_of_the_Desert "Conquest of the Desert") occurred, with the purpose of tripling the Argentine territory by means of the constant confrontations between natives and Criollos in the border,[[81]](#cite_note-89) and the appropriation of the indigenous territories. The first conquest consisted of a series of military incursions into the Pampa and Patagonian territories dominated by the indigenous peoples,[[82]](#cite_note-90) distributing them among the members of the [Sociedad Rural Argentina](/wiki/Sociedad_Rural_Argentina "Sociedad Rural Argentina"), financiers of the expeditions.[[83]](#cite_note-91) The conquest of Chaco lasted up to the end of the century,[[84]](#cite_note-92) since its full ownership of the national economic system only took place when the mere extraction of wood and [tannin](/wiki/Tannin "Tannin") was replaced by the production of [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton").[[85]](#cite_note-93) The Argentine government considered [indigenous people](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Argentina "Indigenous peoples in Argentina") as inferior beings, without the same rights as Criollos and Europeans.[[86]](#cite_note-94)

In 1912, President [Roque Sáenz Peña](/wiki/Roque_S%C3%A1enz_Pe%C3%B1a "Roque Sáenz Peña") enacted [universal and secret male suffrage](/wiki/Saenz_Pe%C3%B1a_Law "Saenz Peña Law"), which allowed [Hipólito Yrigoyen](/wiki/Hip%C3%B3lito_Yrigoyen "Hipólito Yrigoyen"), leader of the [Radical Civic Union](/wiki/Radical_Civic_Union "Radical Civic Union") (or UCR), to win [the 1916 election](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_1916 "Argentine general election, 1916"). He enacted social and economic reforms and extended assistance to small farms and businesses. Argentina stayed neutral during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). The second administration of Yrigoyen faced an economic crisis, precipitated by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso20117–178vol._II-95)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Golpe_de_Estado_en_Argentina_en_1930.jpg/250px-Golpe_de_Estado_en_Argentina_en_1930.jpg)](/wiki/File:Golpe_de_Estado_en_Argentina_en_1930.jpg)

Crowds outside the [Argentine National Congress](/wiki/Argentine_National_Congress "Argentine National Congress") during the [1930 Argentine coup d'état](/wiki/1930_Argentine_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1930 Argentine coup d'état") which marked the start of the [Infamous Decade](/wiki/Infamous_Decade "Infamous Decade")

In 1930, Yrigoyen [was ousted from power](/wiki/1930_Argentine_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1930 Argentine coup d'état") by the military led by [José Félix Uriburu](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_F%C3%A9lix_Uriburu "José Félix Uriburu"). Although Argentina remained among the fifteen richest countries until mid-century,[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoltVan_Zanden2013-80) this [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") marked the start of the steady economic and social decline that pushed the country back into underdevelopment.[[88]](#cite_note-developed-96)

Uriburu ruled for two years; then [Agustín Pedro Justo](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_Pedro_Justo "Agustín Pedro Justo") was elected in a [fraudulent election](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_1931 "Argentine general election, 1931") and signed a controversial [treaty with the United Kingdom](/wiki/Roca-Runciman_Treaty "Roca-Runciman Treaty"). Argentina [stayed neutral during World War II](/wiki/Argentina_in_World_War_II "Argentina in World War II"), a decision that had full British support but was rejected by the United States after the [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor"). In 1943 [a military coup d'état](/wiki/Revolution_of_%2743 "Revolution of '43") led by [General Arturo Rawson](/wiki/Arturo_Rawson "Arturo Rawson") toppled the constitutional government of [Ramón Castillo](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_Castillo "Ramón Castillo"). Under pressure from the United States, later Argentina declared war on the Axis Powers (on 27 March 1945, roughly a month before the [end of World War II in Europe](/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Europe "End of World War II in Europe")).

During the Rawson dictatorship a relatively unknown military colonel named [Juan Perón](/wiki/Juan_Per%C3%B3n "Juan Perón") was named head of the Labour Department. Perón quickly managed to climb the political ladder, being named defence minister in 1944. Being perceived as a political threat by rivals in the military and the conservative camp, he was forced to resign in 1945, and was arrested days later. He was finally released under mounting pressure from both his base and several allied unions.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011181–302vol._II-97) He later became president after a landslide victory over the UCR in the [1946 general election](/wiki/1946_Argentine_general_election "1946 Argentine general election") as the [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(Argentina) "Labour Party (Argentina)") candidate.[[90]](#cite_note-98)

### Peronist years

Main article: [Peronism](/wiki/Peronism "Peronism")

[![Juan Domingo Perón and his wife Eva Perón, 1947.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Juan_y_Eva_Oficial.jpg/250px-Juan_y_Eva_Oficial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Juan_y_Eva_Oficial.jpg)

[Juan Perón](/wiki/Juan_Per%C3%B3n "Juan Perón") and his wife [Eva Perón](/wiki/Eva_Per%C3%B3n "Eva Perón"), 1947

The [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(Argentina) "Labour Party (Argentina)") (later renamed [Justicialist Party](/wiki/Justicialist_Party "Justicialist Party")), the most powerful and influential party in Argentine history, came into power with the rise of Juan Perón to the presidency in 1946. He [nationalised](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") strategic industries and services, improved wages and working conditions, paid the full [external debt](/wiki/External_debt "External debt") and claimed he achieved nearly [full employment](/wiki/Full_employment "Full employment"). He pushed Congress to enact [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") in 1947,[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnes19783-99) and developed a system of social assistance for the most vulnerable sectors of society.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnes1978113ff-100) The economy began to decline in 1950 due in part to government expenditures and the [protectionist](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism") economic policies.[[93]](#cite_note-Reutuers2014-101)

He also engaged in a campaign of political suppression. Anyone who was perceived to be a political dissident or potential rival was subject to threats, physical violence and harassment. The Argentine [intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia "Intelligentsia"), the middle-class, university students, and professors were seen as particularly troublesome. Perón fired over 2,000 university professors and faculty members from all major public education institutions.[[94]](#cite_note-auth-102)

Perón tried to bring most trade and labour unions under his thumb, regularly resorting to violence when needed. For instance, the meat-packers union leader, [Cipriano Reyes](/wiki/Cipriano_Reyes "Cipriano Reyes"), organised strikes in protest against the government after elected labour movement officials were forcefully replaced by Peronist puppets from the [Peronist Party](/wiki/Justicialist_Party "Justicialist Party"). Reyes was soon arrested on charges of terrorism, though the allegations were never substantiated. Reyes, who was never formally charged, was tortured in prison for five years and only released after the regime's downfall in 1955.[[95]](#cite_note-103)

Perón [managed to get reelected in 1951](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_1951 "Argentine general election, 1951"). His wife [Eva Perón](/wiki/Eva_Per%C3%B3n "Eva Perón"), who played a critical role in the party, died of cancer in 1952. As the economy continued to tank, Perón started losing popular support, and came to be seen as a threat to the national process. The Navy took advantage of Perón's withering political power, and [bombed the Plaza de Mayo](/wiki/Bombing_of_Plaza_de_Mayo "Bombing of Plaza de Mayo") in 1955. Perón survived the attack, but a few months later, during the [Liberating Revolution](/wiki/Revoluci%C3%B3n_Libertadora "Revolución Libertadora") coup, he was deposed and went into [exile](/wiki/Exile "Exile") in Spain.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011303–51vol._II-104)

### Revolución Libertadora

Main article: [Revolución Libertadora](/wiki/Revoluci%C3%B3n_Libertadora "Revolución Libertadora")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Plaza-Mayo-bombardeo-1955.JPG/250px-Plaza-Mayo-bombardeo-1955.JPG)](/wiki/File:Plaza-Mayo-bombardeo-1955.JPG)

Civilian casualties after the [air attack and massacre on Plaza de Mayo](/wiki/Bombing_of_Plaza_de_Mayo "Bombing of Plaza de Mayo"), June 1955

The new head of State, [Pedro Eugenio Aramburu](/wiki/Pedro_Eugenio_Aramburu "Pedro Eugenio Aramburu"), [proscribed](/wiki/Proscription "Proscription") Peronism and banned the party from any future elections. [Arturo Frondizi](/wiki/Arturo_Frondizi "Arturo Frondizi") from the [UCR](/wiki/Radical_Civic_Union "Radical Civic Union") won the [1958 general election](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_1958 "Argentine general election, 1958").[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011353–379vol._II-105) He encouraged investment to achieve energetic and industrial self-sufficiency, reversed a chronic [trade deficit](/wiki/Trade_deficit "Trade deficit") and lifted the ban on Peronism; yet his efforts to stay on good terms with both the Peronists and the military earned him the rejection of both and a new coup forced him out.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobben201134-106) Amidst the political turmoil, Senate leader [José María Guido](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Guido "José María Guido") reacted swiftly and applied anti-[power vacuum](/wiki/Power_vacuum "Power vacuum") legislation, ascending to the presidency himself; elections were repealed and Peronism was prohibited once again. [Arturo Illia](/wiki/Arturo_Illia "Arturo Illia") was [elected in 1963](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_1963 "Argentine general election, 1963") and led an increase in prosperity across the board; however he was overthrown in 1966 by another military [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") led by General [Juan Carlos Onganía](/wiki/Juan_Carlos_Ongan%C3%ADa "Juan Carlos Onganía") in the self-proclaimed [Argentine Revolution](/wiki/Argentine_Revolution "Argentine Revolution"), creating a new military government that sought to rule indefinitely.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011381–422vol._II-107)

### Perón's return and death

Following several years of military rule, [Alejandro Agustín Lanusse](/wiki/Alejandro_Agust%C3%ADn_Lanusse "Alejandro Agustín Lanusse") was appointed president by the [military junta](/wiki/Military_junta "Military junta") in 1971. Under increasing political pressure for the return of democracy, Lanusse called for elections in 1973. Perón was banned from running but the Peronist party was allowed to participate.[[100]](#cite_note-108) The presidential elections were won by Perón's surrogate candidate, [Hector Cámpora](/wiki/Hector_C%C3%A1mpora "Hector Cámpora"), a left-wing Peronist, who took office on 25 May 1973. A month later, in June, Perón returned from Spain. One of Cámpora's first presidential actions was to grant amnesty to members of organisations that had carried out political assassinations and terrorist attacks, and to those who had been tried and sentenced to prison by judges. Cámpora's months-long tenure in government was beset by political and social unrest. Over 600 social conflicts, [strikes](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action"), and [factory occupations](/wiki/Workers%27_self-management "Workers' self-management") took place within a single month.[[101]](#cite_note-109) Even though far-left terrorist organisations had suspended their armed struggle, their joining with the [participatory democracy](/wiki/Participatory_democracy "Participatory democracy") process was interpreted as a direct threat by the Peronist right-wing faction.[[102]](#cite_note-110)

Amid a state of political, social, and economic upheaval, Cámpora and Vice President Vicente Solano Lima resigned in July 1973, calling for new elections, but this time with Perón as the Justicialist Party nominee. Perón won the election with his wife [Isabel Perón](/wiki/Isabel_Per%C3%B3n "Isabel Perón") as vice president. Perón's third term was marked by escalating conflict between left and right-wing factions within the Peronist party, as well as the return of armed terror guerrilla groups such as the Guevarist [ERP](/wiki/People%27s_Revolutionary_Army_(Argentina) "People's Revolutionary Army (Argentina)"), leftist Peronist [Montoneros](/wiki/Montoneros "Montoneros"), and the state-backed far-right [Triple A](/wiki/Argentine_Anticommunist_Alliance "Argentine Anticommunist Alliance"). After a series of heart attacks and signs of pneumonia in 1974, Perón's health deteriorated quickly. He suffered a final heart attack on Monday, 1 July 1974, and died at 13:15. He was 78 years old. After his death, [Isabel Perón](/wiki/Isabel_Per%C3%B3n "Isabel Perón"), his wife and vice president, succeeded him in office. During her presidency, a military junta, along with the Peronists' far-right fascist faction, once again became the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). Isabel Perón served as President of Argentina from 1974 until 1976, when she was ousted by the military. Her short presidency was marked by the collapse of Argentine political and social systems, leading to a constitutional crisis that paved the way for a decade of instability, left-wing terrorist guerrilla attacks, and state-sponsored terrorism.[[93]](#cite_note-Reutuers2014-101)[[103]](#cite_note-111)[[104]](#cite_note-112)[[105]](#cite_note-113)[[106]](#cite_note-114)

### National Reorganization Process

Main articles: [National Reorganization Process](/wiki/National_Reorganization_Process "National Reorganization Process") and [Dirty War](/wiki/Dirty_War "Dirty War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Junta_Militar_argentina_1976.png/250px-Junta_Militar_argentina_1976.png)](/wiki/File:Junta_Militar_argentina_1976.png)

The "[first military junta](/wiki/National_Reorganization_Process "National Reorganization Process")" – Admiral [Emilio Massera](/wiki/Emilio_Eduardo_Massera "Emilio Eduardo Massera"), Lieutenant General [Jorge Videla](/wiki/Jorge_Rafael_Videla "Jorge Rafael Videla") and Brigadier General [Orlando Agosti](/wiki/Orlando_Ram%C3%B3n_Agosti "Orlando Ramón Agosti") (from left to right) – observing the [Independence Day](/wiki/Argentine_Declaration_of_Independence "Argentine Declaration of Independence") military parade on [Avenida del Libertador](/wiki/Avenida_del_Libertador_(Buenos_Aires) "Avenida del Libertador (Buenos Aires)"), 9 July 1978

The "Dirty War" (Spanish: *Guerra Sucia*) was part of [Operation Condor](/wiki/Operation_Condor "Operation Condor"), which included the participation of other right-wing dictatorships in the [Southern Cone](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone"). The Dirty War involved [state terrorism](/wiki/State_terrorism "State terrorism") in Argentina and elsewhere in the Southern Cone against political dissidents, with military and security forces employing urban and rural violence against left-wing guerrillas, political dissidents, and anyone believed to be associated with socialism or somehow contrary to the [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism") economic policies of the regime.[[107]](#cite_note-115)[[108]](#cite_note-116)[[109]](#cite_note-117) Victims of the violence in Argentina alone included an estimated 15,000 to 30,000 left-wing activists and militants, including trade unionists, students, journalists, [Marxists](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist"), [Peronist](/wiki/Peronism "Peronism") [guerrillas](/wiki/Guerrilla "Guerrilla"),[[110]](#cite_note-118) and alleged sympathisers. Most of the victims were casualties of [state terrorism](/wiki/State_terrorism "State terrorism"). The opposing guerrillas' victims numbered nearly 500–540 military and police officials[[111]](#cite_note-119) and up to 230 civilians.[[112]](#cite_note-120) Argentina received technical support and military aid from the United States government during the [Johnson](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson"), [Nixon](/wiki/Presidency_of_Richard_Nixon "Presidency of Richard Nixon"), [Ford](/wiki/Presidency_of_Gerald_Ford "Presidency of Gerald Ford"), [Carter](/wiki/Presidency_of_Jimmy_Carter "Presidency of Jimmy Carter"), and [Reagan](/wiki/Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan "Presidency of Ronald Reagan") administrations.

The exact chronology of the [repression](/wiki/Political_repression "Political repression") is still debated, yet the roots of the long political war may have started in 1969 when trade unionists were targeted for assassination by Peronist and Marxist paramilitaries. Individual cases of [state-sponsored terrorism](/wiki/State-sponsored_terrorism "State-sponsored terrorism") against Peronism and the left can be traced back even further to the [Bombing of Plaza de Mayo](/wiki/Bombing_of_Plaza_de_Mayo "Bombing of Plaza de Mayo") in 1955. The [Trelew massacre](/wiki/Trelew_massacre "Trelew massacre") of 1972, the actions of the [Argentine Anticommunist Alliance](/wiki/Argentine_Anticommunist_Alliance "Argentine Anticommunist Alliance") commencing in 1973, and [Isabel Perón](/wiki/Isabel_Per%C3%B3n "Isabel Perón")'s "annihilation decrees" against left-wing guerrillas during *[Operativo Independencia](/wiki/Operativo_Independencia "Operativo Independencia")* (Operation Independence) in 1975, are also possible events signaling the beginning of the Dirty War.[[H]](#cite_note-124)

Onganía shut down Congress, banned all political parties, and dismantled student and worker unions. In 1969, popular discontent led to two massive protests: the *[Cordobazo](/wiki/Cordobazo "Cordobazo")* and the *[Rosariazo](/wiki/Rosariazo "Rosariazo")*. The terrorist guerrilla organisation [Montoneros](/wiki/Montoneros "Montoneros") [kidnapped and executed](/wiki/Assassination_of_Pedro_Eugenio_Aramburu "Assassination of Pedro Eugenio Aramburu") Aramburu.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobben2011127-125) The newly chosen head of government, [Alejandro Agustín Lanusse](/wiki/Alejandro_Agust%C3%ADn_Lanusse "Alejandro Agustín Lanusse"), seeking to ease the growing political pressure, allowed [Héctor José Cámpora](/wiki/H%C3%A9ctor_Jos%C3%A9_C%C3%A1mpora "Héctor José Cámpora") to become the Peronist candidate instead of Perón. Cámpora won the [March 1973 election](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_March_1973 "Argentine general election, March 1973"), issued [pardons](/wiki/Amnesty "Amnesty") for condemned guerrilla members, and then secured Perón's return from his exile in Spain.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011423–65vol._II-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Marines%26Paras_with_POWs_Stanley_1982.JPG/250px-Marines%26Paras_with_POWs_Stanley_1982.JPG)](/wiki/File:Marines%26Paras_with_POWs_Stanley_1982.JPG)

Argentine POWs after the surrender in 1982

On the day Perón returned to Argentina, the clash between Peronist internal factions—[right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing "Right-wing") union leaders and [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing "Left-wing") youth from the Montoneros—resulted in the [Ezeiza Massacre](/wiki/Ezeiza_Massacre "Ezeiza Massacre"). Overwhelmed by political violence, Cámpora resigned and Perón won the following [September 1973 election](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_September_1973 "Argentine general election, September 1973") with his third wife [Isabel](/wiki/Isabel_Per%C3%B3n "Isabel Perón") as vice-president. He [expelled Montoneros from the party](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Montoneros_from_Plaza_de_Mayo "Expulsion of Montoneros from Plaza de Mayo")[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobben201176–77-127) and they became once again a clandestine organisation. [José López Rega](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_L%C3%B3pez_Rega "José López Rega") organised the [Argentine Anticommunist Alliance](/wiki/Argentine_Anticommunist_Alliance "Argentine Anticommunist Alliance") (AAA) to fight against them and the [People's Revolutionary Army](/wiki/People%27s_Revolutionary_Army_(Argentina) "People's Revolutionary Army (Argentina)") (ERP).[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndersonSloan200940–41-128)[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2016167-129)

Perón died in July 1974 and was succeeded by his wife, who signed a secret decree empowering the military and the police to "annihilate" the left-wing subversion,[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobben2011145-130) [stopping ERP's attempt](/wiki/Operation_Independence "Operation Independence") to start a rural insurgence in Tucumán province.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobben2011148-131) [Isabel Perón was ousted](/wiki/March_1976_coup "March 1976 coup") one year later by a junta of the combined armed forces, led by army general [Jorge Rafael Videla](/wiki/Jorge_Rafael_Videla "Jorge Rafael Videla"). They initiated the [National Reorganization Process](/wiki/National_Reorganization_Process "National Reorganization Process"), often shortened to *Proceso*.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011467–504vol._II-132)

The *Proceso* shut down Congress, removed the judges on the Supreme Court, banned political parties and unions, and resorted to employing the [forced disappearance](/wiki/Forced_disappearance "Forced disappearance") of suspected guerrilla members including individuals suspected of being associated with the left-wing. By the end of 1976, the Montoneros had lost nearly 2,000 members and by 1977, the ERP was completely subdued. Nevertheless, the severely weakened Montoneros launched a counterattack in 1979, which was quickly put down, effectively ending the guerrilla threat and securing the junta's position in power.[[124]](#cite_note-133)

In March 1982, an Argentine force took control of the British territory of [South Georgia](/wiki/South_Georgia "South Georgia") and, on 2 April, Argentina [invaded the Falkland Islands](/wiki/Operation_Rosario "Operation Rosario"). The United Kingdom dispatched a task force to regain possession. Argentina surrendered on 14 June and its forces were taken home. Street riots in Buenos Aires followed the humiliating defeat and the military leadership stood down.[[125]](#cite_note-134)[[126]](#cite_note-135) [Reynaldo Bignone](/wiki/Reynaldo_Bignone "Reynaldo Bignone") replaced Galtieri and began to organise the transition to democratic governance.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011505–32vol._II-136)

### Return to democracy

Main articles: [Presidency of Raúl Alfonsín](/wiki/Presidency_of_Ra%C3%BAl_Alfons%C3%ADn "Presidency of Raúl Alfonsín") and [Argentine economic crisis (1999–2002)](/wiki/Argentine_economic_crisis_(1999%E2%80%932002) "Argentine economic crisis (1999–2002)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/De_la_R%C3%BAa_con_Menem.jpg/250px-De_la_R%C3%BAa_con_Menem.jpg)](/wiki/File:De_la_R%C3%BAa_con_Menem.jpg)

[Menem](/wiki/Carlos_Menem "Carlos Menem") (left) with [Fernando de la Rúa](/wiki/Fernando_de_la_R%C3%BAa "Fernando de la Rúa") on 10 December 1999 just after the latter became president

[Raúl Alfonsín](/wiki/Ra%C3%BAl_Alfons%C3%ADn "Raúl Alfonsín") won the [1983 elections](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_1983 "Argentine general election, 1983") campaigning for the prosecution of those responsible for [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") violations during the *Proceso*: the [Trial of the Juntas](/wiki/Trial_of_the_Juntas "Trial of the Juntas") and other martial courts sentenced all the coup's leaders but, under military pressure, he also enacted the [Full Stop](/wiki/Full_Stop_Law "Full Stop Law") and [Due Obedience](/wiki/Law_of_Due_Obedience "Law of Due Obedience") laws,[[128]](#cite_note-137)[[129]](#cite_note-138) which halted prosecutions further down the [chain of command](/wiki/Chain_of_command "Chain of command"). The worsening economic crisis and [hyperinflation](/wiki/Hyperinflation "Hyperinflation") reduced his popular support and the Peronist [Carlos Menem](/wiki/Carlos_Menem "Carlos Menem") won the [1989 election](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_1989 "Argentine general election, 1989"). Soon after, [riots forced Alfonsín to an early resignation](/wiki/1989_riots_in_Argentina "1989 riots in Argentina").[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011533–49vol._II-139)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Crisis_20_diciembre_2001.jpg/250px-Crisis_20_diciembre_2001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crisis_20_diciembre_2001.jpg)

Civil unrest on [Plaza de Mayo](/wiki/Plaza_de_Mayo "Plaza de Mayo") during the [Argentinazo riots](/wiki/December_2001_riots_in_Argentina "December 2001 riots in Argentina"), which forced De La Rúa to resign on 21 December 2001

Menem embraced and enacted [neoliberal](/wiki/Neo-liberalism "Neo-liberalism") policies:[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinPion-Berlin20066-140) a [fixed exchange rate](/wiki/Argentine_Currency_Board "Argentine Currency Board"), business [deregulation](/wiki/Deregulation "Deregulation"), [privatisations](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization"), and the dismantling of [protectionist](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism") barriers normalised the economy in the short term. He pardoned the officers who had been sentenced during Alfonsín's government. The [1994 Constitutional Amendment](/wiki/1994_amendment_of_the_Argentine_Constitution "1994 amendment of the Argentine Constitution") allowed Menem to [be elected for a second term](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_1995 "Argentine general election, 1995"). With the economy beginning to decline in 1995, and with increasing unemployment and recession,[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinPion-Berlin20069-141) the UCR, led by [Fernando de la Rúa](/wiki/Fernando_de_la_R%C3%BAa "Fernando de la Rúa"), returned to the presidency in the [1999 elections](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_1999 "Argentine general election, 1999").[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011551–573vol._II-142)

De la Rúa left Menem's economic plan in effect despite the worsening crisis, which led to growing social discontent.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinPion-Berlin20069-141) Massive [capital flight](/wiki/Capital_flight "Capital flight") from the country was responded to with a [freezing of bank accounts](/wiki/Corralito "Corralito"), generating further turmoil. The [December 2001 riots](/wiki/December_2001_riots_in_Argentina "December 2001 riots in Argentina") forced him to resign.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011575–87vol._II-143) Congress appointed [Eduardo Duhalde](/wiki/Eduardo_Duhalde "Eduardo Duhalde") as acting president, who revoked the fixed exchange rate established by Menem,[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinPion-Berlin200612-144) causing many working- and middle-class Argentines to lose a significant portion of their savings. By late 2002, the economic crisis began to recede, but the assassination of two *[piqueteros](/wiki/Piquetero "Piquetero")* by the police caused political unrest, prompting Duhalde to move elections forward.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinPion-Berlin200613-145) [Néstor Kirchner](/wiki/N%C3%A9stor_Kirchner "Néstor Kirchner") was [elected as the new president](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_2003 "Argentine general election, 2003"). On 26 May 2003, he was sworn in.[[137]](#cite_note-146)[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011587–95vol._II-147)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Cristina_con_baston_de_mando_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Cristina_con_baston_de_mando_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cristina_con_baston_de_mando_(cropped).jpg)

[Kirchner](/wiki/N%C3%A9stor_Kirchner "Néstor Kirchner") and his wife [Cristina Kirchner](/wiki/Cristina_Kirchner "Cristina Kirchner") just after [her presidency](/wiki/Presidency_of_Cristina_Fern%C3%A1ndez_de_Kirchner "Presidency of Cristina Fernández de Kirchner") began, 10 December 2007

Boosting the [neo-Keynesian](/wiki/Neo-Keynesianism "Neo-Keynesianism") economic policies[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinPion-Berlin200613-145) laid by Duhalde, Kirchner ended the economic crisis attaining significant fiscal and trade surpluses, and rapid [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") growth.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinPion-Berlin200616-148) Under his administration, Argentina [restructured its defaulted debt](/wiki/Argentine_debt_restructuring "Argentine debt restructuring") with an unprecedented discount of about 70% on most bonds, paid off debts with the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"),[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinPion-Berlin200615-149) purged the military of officers with dubious human rights records,[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinPion-Berlin200614-150) [nullified and voided](/wiki/Void_(law) "Void (law)") the Full Stop and Due Obedience laws,[[142]](#cite_note-151)[[I]](#cite_note-153) ruled them as unconstitutional, and resumed legal prosecution of the Junta's crimes. He did not run for reelection, promoting instead the candidacy of his wife, senator [Cristina Kirchner](/wiki/Cristina_Fern%C3%A1ndez_de_Kirchner "Cristina Fernández de Kirchner") who won the [2007](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_2007 "Argentine general election, 2007") and [2011](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_2011 "Argentine general election, 2011") elections.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011597–626vol._II-154) During [Kirchner's presidency](/wiki/Presidency_of_Cristina_Fern%C3%A1ndez_de_Kirchner "Presidency of Cristina Fernández de Kirchner") she promoted foreign relations with countries such as Venezuela, Iran, and Cuba, whilst US and UK relations were increasingly strained. Despite increased renewable energy production and subsidies during her tenure, the overall economy had been sluggish since 2011.[[145]](#cite_note-155)

On 22 November 2015, after a tie in the first round of [presidential elections on 25 October](/wiki/Argentine_general_election,_2015 "Argentine general election, 2015"), [centre-right coalition](/wiki/Juntos_por_el_Cambio "Juntos por el Cambio") candidate [Mauricio Macri](/wiki/Mauricio_Macri "Mauricio Macri") won the first [ballotage](/wiki/Ballotage_in_Argentina "Ballotage in Argentina") in Argentina's history, beating [Front for Victory](/wiki/Front_for_Victory "Front for Victory") candidate [Daniel Scioli](/wiki/Daniel_Scioli "Daniel Scioli") and becoming president-elect.[[146]](#cite_note-156) Macri was the first democratically elected non-[peronist](/wiki/Justicialist_Party "Justicialist Party") president since 1916 that managed to complete his term in office without being overthrown.[[147]](#cite_note-157) He took office on 10 December 2015 and inherited an economy with a high inflation rate and in a poor shape.[[148]](#cite_note-158) In April 2016, the [Macri government](/wiki/Presidency_of_Mauricio_Macri "Presidency of Mauricio Macri") introduced neoliberal austerity measures intended to tackle [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") and overblown public deficits.[[149]](#cite_note-159) Under Macri's administration, economic recovery remained elusive with GDP shrinking 3.4%, inflation totalling 240%, billions of US dollars issued in sovereign debt, and mass poverty increasing by the end of his term.[[150]](#cite_note-160)[[151]](#cite_note-161) He ran for reelection in 2019 but lost by nearly eight percentage points to [Alberto Fernández](/wiki/Alberto_Fern%C3%A1ndez "Alberto Fernández"), the Justicialist Party candidate.[[152]](#cite_note-162)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Javier_Milei_y_Alberto_Fern%C3%A1ndez1.jpg/250px-Javier_Milei_y_Alberto_Fern%C3%A1ndez1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Javier_Milei_y_Alberto_Fern%C3%A1ndez1.jpg)

[Javier Milei](/wiki/Javier_Milei "Javier Milei") receiving [the presidency](/wiki/Presidency_of_Javier_Milei "Presidency of Javier Milei") from [Fernández](/wiki/Alberto_Fern%C3%A1ndez "Alberto Fernández") on [10 December 2023](/wiki/Inauguration_of_Javier_Milei "Inauguration of Javier Milei")

Fernández and vice president Cristina Kirchner took office in December 2019,[[153]](#cite_note-163) just months before the [COVID-19 pandemic hit Argentina](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Argentina "COVID-19 pandemic in Argentina") and among accusations of [corruption](/wiki/Corruption "Corruption"), [bribery](/wiki/Bribery "Bribery") and [misuse of public funds during Néstor and Cristina Kirchner's presidencies](/wiki/The_Route_of_the_K-Money "The Route of the K-Money").[[154]](#cite_note-164)[[155]](#cite_note-Mercopress-165) In November 2021, the centre-left coalition of Argentina's ruling Peronist party, [Frente de Todos](/wiki/Frente_de_Todos "Frente de Todos") (Front for Everyone), lost its majority in Congress, for the first time in almost 40 years, in midterm [legislative elections](/wiki/2021_Argentine_legislative_election "2021 Argentine legislative election"). The election victory of the centre-right coalition, [Juntos por el Cambio](/wiki/Juntos_por_el_Cambio "Juntos por el Cambio") (Together for Change) limited Fernández' power during his final two years in office. Losing control of the Senate made it difficult for him to make key appointments, including to the judiciary. It also forced him to negotiate with the opposition on every initiative send to the legislature.[[156]](#cite_note-166)[[157]](#cite_note-167)

In April 2023, Fernández announced that he would not seek reelection in the next [presidential election](/wiki/2023_Argentine_general_election "2023 Argentine general election").[[158]](#cite_note-168) The November [2023 election](/wiki/2023_Argentine_general_election "2023 Argentine general election") run-off ended in a win for libertarian outsider [Javier Milei](/wiki/Javier_Milei "Javier Milei") with 55.7% of the vote against 44.4% to ruling coalition candidate [Sergio Massa](/wiki/Sergio_Massa "Sergio Massa").[[159]](#cite_note-169) [Milei's presidency](/wiki/Presidency_of_Javier_Milei "Presidency of Javier Milei") began on [10 December 2023](/wiki/Inauguration_of_Javier_Milei "Inauguration of Javier Milei").[[160]](#cite_note-170) In October 2025, President Javier Milei's party, [La Libertad Avanza](/wiki/La_Libertad_Avanza "La Libertad Avanza"), won a landslide victory in [midterm elections](/wiki/2025_Argentine_legislative_election "2025 Argentine legislative election"), making it easier for Milei to push ahead with his programme of radical spending cuts and free-market reforms.[[161]](#cite_note-171)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Argentina](/wiki/Geography_of_Argentina "Geography of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Argentina_topo_blank.jpg/250px-Argentina_topo_blank.jpg)](/wiki/File:Argentina_topo_blank.jpg)

Topographical map of Argentina

With a mainland surface area of 2,780,400 km2 (1,073,518 sq mi),[[A]](#cite_note-excl_area-9) Argentina is located in [southern South America](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone"), sharing land borders with Chile across the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") to the west;[[162]](#cite_note-172) Bolivia and Paraguay to the north; Brazil to the northeast, [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") and the [South Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/South_Atlantic_Ocean "South Atlantic Ocean") to the east;[[163]](#cite_note-igngeo-173) and the [Drake Passage](/wiki/Drake_Passage "Drake Passage") to the south;[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKinney19936FearnsFearns200531-174) for an overall land border length of 9,376 km (5,826 mi). Its coastal border over the [Río de la Plata](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Río de la Plata") and [South Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/South_Atlantic_Ocean "South Atlantic Ocean") is 5,117 km (3,180 mi) long.[[163]](#cite_note-igngeo-173)

Argentina's highest point is [Aconcagua](/wiki/Aconcagua "Aconcagua") in the [Mendoza province](/wiki/Mendoza_province "Mendoza province") (6,959 m (22,831 ft) above sea level),[[165]](#cite_note-ignmax-175) also the highest point in the [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") and [Western Hemispheres](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere").[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoung200552-176) The lowest point is [Laguna del Carbón](/wiki/Laguna_del_Carb%C3%B3n "Laguna del Carbón") in the *San Julián Great Depression* [Santa Cruz province](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_province,_Argentina "Santa Cruz province, Argentina") (−105 m (−344 ft) below sea level,[[165]](#cite_note-ignmax-175) also the lowest point in the Southern and Western Hemispheres, and the seventh lowest point on Earth).[[167]](#cite_note-177)

The northernmost point is at the confluence of the [Grande de San Juan](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_Grande_de_San_Juan "Río Grande de San Juan") and Mojinete rivers in [Jujuy province](/wiki/Jujuy_province "Jujuy province"); the southernmost is [Cape San Pío](/wiki/Cape_San_P%C3%ADo "Cape San Pío") in [Tierra del Fuego province](/wiki/Tierra_del_Fuego_province,_Argentina "Tierra del Fuego province, Argentina"); the easternmost is northeast of [Bernardo de Irigoyen, Misiones](/wiki/Bernardo_de_Irigoyen,_Misiones "Bernardo de Irigoyen, Misiones") and the westernmost is within [Los Glaciares National Park](/wiki/Los_Glaciares_National_Park "Los Glaciares National Park") in Santa Cruz province.[[163]](#cite_note-igngeo-173) The maximum north–south distance is 3,694 km (2,295 mi), while the maximum east–west one is 1,423 km (884 mi).[[163]](#cite_note-igngeo-173)

Some of the major rivers are the [Paraná](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_River "Paraná River"), [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_River "Uruguay River")—which join to form the Río de la Plata, [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_River "Paraguay River"), [Salado](/wiki/Salado_River,_Argentina "Salado River, Argentina"), [Negro](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_Negro_River,_Argentina "Río Negro River, Argentina"), [Santa Cruz](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_River,_Argentina "Santa Cruz River, Argentina"), [Pilcomayo](/wiki/Pilcomayo_River "Pilcomayo River"), [Bermejo](/wiki/Bermejo_River "Bermejo River") and [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado_River,_Argentina "Colorado River, Argentina").[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford20065,_7–8,_51,_175-178) These rivers are discharged into the [Argentine Sea](/wiki/Argentine_Sea "Argentine Sea"), the shallow area of the Atlantic Ocean over the [Patagonian Shelf](/wiki/Patagonian_Shelf "Patagonian Shelf"), an unusually wide [continental platform](/wiki/Continental_platform "Continental platform").[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford20068-179) Its waters are influenced by two major ocean currents: the warm [Brazil Current](/wiki/Brazil_Current "Brazil Current") and the cold [Falklands Current](/wiki/Falklands_Current "Falklands Current").[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford200618-180)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Environment of Argentina](/wiki/Environment_of_Argentina "Environment of Argentina")

[![Mountain tops, with clouds shown.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Aconcagua2016.jpg/250px-Aconcagua2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aconcagua2016.jpg)

[Aconcagua](/wiki/Aconcagua "Aconcagua") is the [highest mountain](/wiki/Extremes_of_Altitude "Extremes of Altitude") outside of Asia, at 6,960.8 metres (22,837 ft), and the highest point in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere")[[171]](#cite_note-UNC-Sigma-181)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Pncardones.jpg/250px-Pncardones.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pncardones.jpg)

[Los Cardones National Park](/wiki/Los_Cardones_National_Park "Los Cardones National Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Cockspur_Coral_Tree_%28Erythrina_crista-galli%29.jpg/250px-Cockspur_Coral_Tree_%28Erythrina_crista-galli%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cockspur_Coral_Tree_(Erythrina_crista-galli).jpg)

The ceibo flower (*[Erythrina crista-galli](/wiki/Erythrina_crista-galli "Erythrina crista-galli")*) is the national flower and tree of Argentina by Decree No. 13,847/42[[172]](#cite_note-182)

Argentina is one of the most [biodiverse](/wiki/Biodiverse "Biodiverse") countries in the world[[173]](#cite_note-cbd-183) hosting one of the greatest [ecosystem](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") varieties in the world: 15 continental zones, 2 marine zones, and the Antarctic region are all represented in its territory.[[173]](#cite_note-cbd-183) This huge ecosystem variety has led to a biological diversity that is among the world's largest:[[173]](#cite_note-cbd-183)[[174]](#cite_note-wcmc-184) 9,372 catalogued [vascular plant](/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant") species (ranked 24th);[[J]](#cite_note-185) 1,038 catalogued bird species (ranked 14th);[[K]](#cite_note-186) 375 catalogued [mammal](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") species (ranked 12th);[[L]](#cite_note-187) 338 catalogued [reptilian](/wiki/Reptile "Reptile") species (ranked 16th); and 162 catalogued [amphibian](/wiki/Amphibian "Amphibian") species (ranked 19th).

In Argentina [forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") is around 10% of the total land area, equivalent to 28,573,000 hectares (ha) of forest in 2020, down from 35,204,000 hectares (ha) in 1990. In 2020, naturally regenerating forest covered 27,137,000 hectares (ha) and planted forest covered 1,436,000 hectares (ha). Of the naturally regenerating forest 0% was reported to be [primary forest](/wiki/Primary_forest "Primary forest") (consisting of native tree species with no clearly visible indications of human activity) and around 7% of the forest area was found within protected areas. For the year 2015, 0% of the forest area was reported to be under [public ownership](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership"), 4% [private ownership](/wiki/Private_property "Private property") and 96% with ownership listed as other or unknown.[[175]](#cite_note-188)[[176]](#cite_note-189)

The original [pampa](/wiki/Pampa "Pampa") had virtually no trees; some imported species such as the [American sycamore](/wiki/Platanus_occidentalis "Platanus occidentalis") or [eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus") are present along roads or in towns and country estates (*estancias*). The only tree-like plant native to the pampa is the evergreen [Ombú](/wiki/Omb%C3%BA "Ombú"). The surface soils of the pampa are a deep black colour, primarily [mollisols](/wiki/Mollisols "Mollisols"), known commonly as *humus*. This makes the region one of the most agriculturally productive on Earth; however, this is also responsible for decimating much of the original ecosystem, to make way for commercial agriculture.[[177]](#cite_note-190) The western pampas receive less rainfall, this *dry pampa* is a plain of short grasses or [steppe](/wiki/Steppe "Steppe").[[178]](#cite_note-191)[[179]](#cite_note-192)

The [National Parks of Argentina](/wiki/National_Parks_of_Argentina "National Parks of Argentina") make up a network of 35 [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park") in Argentina. The parks cover a very varied set of terrains and [biotopes](/wiki/Biotope "Biotope"), from [Baritú National Park](/wiki/Barit%C3%BA_National_Park "Baritú National Park") on the northern border with [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia") to [Tierra del Fuego National Park](/wiki/Tierra_del_Fuego_National_Park "Tierra del Fuego National Park") in the far south of the continent. The [Administración de Parques Nacionales](/wiki/Administraci%C3%B3n_de_Parques_Nacionales "Administración de Parques Nacionales") (National Parks Administration) is the agency that preserves and manages these national parks along with [Natural monuments](/wiki/Natural_monument "Natural monument") and [National Reserves](/wiki/National_Reserve "National Reserve") within the country.[[180]](#cite_note-193) Argentina had a 2018 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 7.21/10, ranking it 47th globally out of 172 countries.[[181]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-194)

### Climate

Main articles: [Climate of Argentina](/wiki/Climate_of_Argentina "Climate of Argentina") and [Climatic regions of Argentina](/wiki/Climatic_regions_of_Argentina "Climatic regions of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_ARG_1991%E2%80%932020.svg/250px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_ARG_1991%E2%80%932020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_ARG_1991%E2%80%932020.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") in Argentina

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Perito_Moreno_%2839986110524%29.jpg/250px-Perito_Moreno_%2839986110524%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Perito_Moreno_(39986110524).jpg)

Argentina features geographical locations such as this glacier, known as the [Perito Moreno Glacier](/wiki/Perito_Moreno_Glacier "Perito Moreno Glacier")[[182]](#cite_note-195)
