In general, Argentina has four main climate types: warm [humid subtropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate"), moderate humid subtropical, [arid](/wiki/Desert_climate "Desert climate"), and [cold](/wiki/Cold_desert "Cold desert"), all determined by the expanse across latitude, range in altitude, and relief features.[[183]](#cite_note-arggov-196)[[184]](#cite_note-197) Although the most populated areas are generally [temperate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate"), Argentina has an exceptional amount of climate diversity,[[185]](#cite_note-FAO-198) ranging from [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropical "Subtropical") in the north to [polar](/wiki/Polar_climate "Polar climate") in the far south.[[186]](#cite_note-199) Consequently, there is a wide variety of [biomes](/wiki/Biomes "Biomes") in the country, including [Subtropical rainforests](/wiki/Subtropical_rainforests "Subtropical rainforests"), [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_Pampas "Semi-arid Pampas") and [arid](/wiki/Desert "Desert") regions, [temperate plains](/wiki/Pampas "Pampas") in the [Pampas](/wiki/Pampas "Pampas"), and cold [subantarctic](/wiki/Subantarctic "Subantarctic") in the south.[[187]](#cite_note-Fernandez-200) The average annual precipitation ranges from 150 millimetres (6 in) in the driest parts of [Patagonia](/wiki/Patagonia "Patagonia") to over 2,000 millimetres (79 in) in the westernmost parts of Patagonia and the northeastern parts of the country.[[185]](#cite_note-FAO-198) Mean annual temperatures range from 5 °C (41 °F) in the far south to 25 °C (77 °F) in the north.[[185]](#cite_note-FAO-198)

Major wind currents include the cool [Pampero Winds](/wiki/Pampero_Winds "Pampero Winds") blowing on the flat plains of Patagonia and the Pampas; following the cold front, warm currents blow from the north in middle and late winter, creating mild conditions.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMenuttiMenutti198069-201)
The [Sudestada](/wiki/Sudestada "Sudestada") usually moderates cold temperatures but brings very heavy rains, rough seas and [coastal flooding](/wiki/Coastal_flooding "Coastal flooding"). It is most common in late autumn and winter along the central coast and in the Río de la Plata estuary.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMenuttiMenutti198069-201)
The [Zonda](/wiki/Zonda_wind "Zonda wind"), a [hot dry wind](/wiki/Foehn_wind "Foehn wind"), affects Cuyo and the central Pampas. Squeezed of all moisture during the 6,000 m (19,685 ft) descent from the Andes, Zonda winds can blow for hours with gusts up to 120 km/h (75 mph), fuelling wildfires and causing damage; between June and November, when the Zonda blows, snowstorms and [blizzard](/wiki/Blizzard "Blizzard") (*viento blanco*) conditions usually affect higher elevations.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMenuttiMenutti198053-202)

[Climate change in Argentina](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Argentina "Climate change in Argentina") is predicted to have significant effects on the living conditions in Argentina.[[190]](#cite_note-cambioclimatico2009-203): 30 The [climate of Argentina](/wiki/Climate_of_Argentina "Climate of Argentina") is changing with regards to precipitation patterns and temperatures. The highest increases in precipitation (from the period 1960–2010) have occurred in the eastern parts of the country. The increase in precipitation has led to more variability in precipitation from year to year in the northern parts of the country, with a higher risk of prolonged [droughts](/wiki/Droughts "Droughts"), disfavouring agriculture in these [regions](/wiki/Regions "Regions").

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of Argentina](/wiki/Politics_of_Argentina "Politics of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Javier_Milei_in_pull-aside_meeting_at_the_United_Nations_Headquarters_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Javier_Milei_in_pull-aside_meeting_at_the_United_Nations_Headquarters_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Javier_Milei_in_pull-aside_meeting_at_the_United_Nations_Headquarters_(3x4_cropped).jpg)

[Javier Milei](/wiki/Javier_Milei "Javier Milei")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Argentina "President of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Victoria_villarruel_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Victoria_villarruel_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_villarruel_2025_(cropped).jpg)

[Victoria Villarruel](/wiki/Victoria_Villarruel "Victoria Villarruel")  
[Vice President](/wiki/Vice_President_of_Argentina "Vice President of Argentina")

In the 20th century, Argentina experienced significant political turmoil and democratic reversals.[[191]](#cite_note-Robinson-204)[[192]](#cite_note-LevitskyMurillo-205) Between 1930 and 1976, the [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Argentine_Republic "Armed Forces of the Argentine Republic") overthrew six governments in Argentina;[[192]](#cite_note-LevitskyMurillo-205) and the country alternated periods of democracy (1912–1930, 1946–1955, and 1973–1976) with periods of restricted democracy and [military rule](/wiki/Military_regime "Military regime").[[191]](#cite_note-Robinson-204) Following a [transition](/wiki/Transition_to_democracy "Transition to democracy") that began in 1983,[[193]](#cite_note-Anderson-206) full-scale democracy in Argentina was reestablished.[[191]](#cite_note-Robinson-204)[[192]](#cite_note-LevitskyMurillo-205) Argentina's democracy endured through the [2001–02 crisis](/wiki/1998%E2%80%932002_Argentine_great_depression "1998–2002 Argentine great depression") and to the present day; it is regarded as more robust than both its pre-1983 predecessors and other democracies in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").[[192]](#cite_note-LevitskyMurillo-205) According to [International IDEA](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance")’s Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Argentina performs in the mid-range on overall democratic measures, with particular strengths in freedom of religion, inclusive suffrage, and civic engagement.[[194]](#cite_note-207) Moreover, according to the [V-Dem Democracy indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_indices "V-Dem Democracy indices"), Argentina in 2023 was the second most [electoral democratic country in Latin America](/wiki/Democracy_in_Latin_America_and_the_Caribbean "Democracy in Latin America and the Caribbean").[[195]](#cite_note-vdem_dataset-208)

### Government

Main articles: [Government of Argentina](/wiki/Government_of_Argentina "Government of Argentina") and [Ministries of the Argentine Republic](/wiki/Ministries_of_the_Argentine_Republic "Ministries of the Argentine Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Casa_Rosada%2C_Buenos_Aires%2C_Argentina.jpg/250px-Casa_Rosada%2C_Buenos_Aires%2C_Argentina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casa_Rosada,_Buenos_Aires,_Argentina.jpg)

[Casa Rosada](/wiki/Casa_Rosada "Casa Rosada") in Buenos Aires, workplace of the [Argentine president](/wiki/President_of_Argentina "President of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Congreso_Nacional_Buenos_Aires.jpg/250px-Congreso_Nacional_Buenos_Aires.jpg)](/wiki/File:Congreso_Nacional_Buenos_Aires.jpg)

The [National Congress](/wiki/National_Congress_of_Argentina "National Congress of Argentina") is composed of the [Senate](/wiki/Argentine_Senate "Argentine Senate") and the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Argentine_Chamber_of_Deputies "Argentine Chamber of Deputies").[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._63-209)

Argentina is a [federal](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") constitutional republic and [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy").[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._1-210) The government is regulated by a system of [checks and balances](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers") defined by the [Constitution of Argentina](/wiki/Constitution_of_Argentina "Constitution of Argentina"), the country's supreme legal document. The [seat of government](/wiki/Seat_of_government "Seat of government") is the city of [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"), as designated by [Congress](/wiki/Argentine_National_Congress "Argentine National Congress").[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._3-211) Suffrage is [universal](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"), [equal](/wiki/Equal_suffrage "Equal suffrage"), [secret](/wiki/Secret_ballot "Secret ballot") and [mandatory](/wiki/Compulsory_voting "Compulsory voting").[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._37-212)[[M]](#cite_note-214)

The federal government is composed of three branches. The [Legislative](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") branch consists of the [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") Congress, made up of the [Senate](/wiki/Argentine_Senate "Argentine Senate") and the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Argentine_Chamber_of_Deputies "Argentine Chamber of Deputies"). The Congress makes [federal law](/wiki/Federal_law "Federal law"), [declares war](/wiki/Declaration_of_war "Declaration of war"), approves [treaties](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty") and has the [power of the purse](/wiki/Power_of_the_purse "Power of the purse") and of [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment "Impeachment"), by which it can remove sitting members of the government.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._53,_59,_75-215) The Chamber of Deputies represents the people and has 257 voting members elected to a four-year term. Seats are apportioned among the provinces by population every tenth year.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._45,_47,_50-216) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Argentina&action=edit) ten provinces have just five deputies while the [Buenos Aires Province](/wiki/Buenos_Aires_Province "Buenos Aires Province"), being the most populous one, has 70. The Chamber of Senators represents the provinces, and has 72 members elected [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") to six-year terms, with each province having three seats; one-third of Senate seats are up for election every other year.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._54,_56-217) At least one-third of the candidates presented by the parties must be women.

In the [Executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") branch, the [President](/wiki/President_of_Argentina "President of Argentina") is the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the military, can [veto](/wiki/Veto "Veto") [legislative bills](/wiki/Bill_(law) "Bill (law)") before they become law—subject to Congressional override—and appoints the [members of the Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Argentina "Cabinet of Argentina") and other officers, who administer and enforce federal laws and policies.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._99-218) The President is elected [directly](/wiki/Direct_vote "Direct vote") by the vote of the people, serves a four-year term and may be elected to office no more than twice in a row.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._90-219)

The [Judicial](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") branch includes the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Argentina "Supreme Court of Argentina") and lower [federal courts](/wiki/Law_of_Argentina "Law of Argentina") interpret laws and [overturn those](/wiki/Judicial_review "Judicial review") they find [unconstitutional](/wiki/Constitutionality "Constitutionality").[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._116-220) The Judicial is independent of the Executive and the Legislative. The Supreme Court has seven members appointed by the President—subject to Senate approval—who serve for life. The lower courts' judges are proposed by the [Council of Magistracy](/wiki/Council_of_Magistracy_of_the_Nation "Council of Magistracy of the Nation") (a secretariat composed of representatives of judges, lawyers, researchers, the Executive and the Legislative), and appointed by the president on Senate approval.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._99,_114-221)

### Provinces

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Map_of_Argentina_with_provinces_names_en.png/330px-Map_of_Argentina_with_provinces_names_en.png)

Provinces of Argentina. Click to explore.

Main article: [Provinces of Argentina](/wiki/Provinces_of_Argentina "Provinces of Argentina")

Argentina is a federation of twenty-three provinces and one [autonomous city](/wiki/Autonomous_city "Autonomous city"), Buenos Aires. Provinces are divided for administration purposes into [departments](/wiki/Departments_of_Argentina "Departments of Argentina") and [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Argentina "Municipalities of Argentina"), except for Buenos Aires Province, which is divided into [partidos](/wiki/Partidos_of_Buenos_Aires "Partidos of Buenos Aires"). The City of Buenos Aires is divided into [communes](/wiki/Barrios_and_Communes_of_Buenos_Aires "Barrios and Communes of Buenos Aires").

Provinces hold all the power that they chose not to delegate to the federal government;[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._121-222) they must be representative republics and must not contradict the Constitution.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._5–6-223) Beyond this they are fully autonomous: they enact their own constitutions,[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._123-224) freely organise their local governments,[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._122-225) and own and manage their natural and financial resources.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._124–125-226) Some provinces have bicameral legislatures, while others have [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") ones.[[N]](#cite_note-228)

[La Pampa](/wiki/La_Pampa_Province "La Pampa Province") and Chaco became provinces in 1951. Misiones did so in 1953, and [Formosa](/wiki/Formosa_Province "Formosa Province"), [Neuquén](/wiki/Neuqu%C3%A9n_Province "Neuquén Province"), [Río Negro](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_Negro_Province "Río Negro Province"), [Chubut](/wiki/Chubut_Province "Chubut Province") and Santa Cruz, in 1955. The last national territory, Tierra del Fuego, became the [Tierra del Fuego, Antártida e Islas del Atlántico Sur Province](/wiki/Tierra_del_Fuego,_Ant%C3%A1rtida_e_Islas_del_Atl%C3%A1ntico_Sur_Province "Tierra del Fuego, Antártida e Islas del Atlántico Sur Province") in 1990.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERey_Balmaceda199519-229) It has three components, although two are nominal because they are not under Argentine sovereignty. The first is the Argentine part of Tierra del Fuego; the second is an area of Antarctica claimed by Argentina that overlaps with similar areas claimed by the UK and Chile; the third comprises the two disputed British Overseas Territories of the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands") and [South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands](/wiki/South_Georgia_and_the_South_Sandwich_Islands "South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands").[[215]](#cite_note-Duggan_and_Lewis-230)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Argentina](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Argentina "Foreign relations of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/BRICS_members_and_guest_at_the_6th_BRICS_summit_2014.jpg/250px-BRICS_members_and_guest_at_the_6th_BRICS_summit_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:BRICS_members_and_guest_at_the_6th_BRICS_summit_2014.jpg)

[Cristina Kirchner](/wiki/Cristina_Kirchner "Cristina Kirchner") alongside the members of [BRICS](/wiki/BRICS "BRICS") and [Union of South American Nations](/wiki/Union_of_South_American_Nations "Union of South American Nations") in 2014

Foreign policy is handled by the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs, International Trade and Worship](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs,_International_Trade_and_Worship "Ministry of Foreign Affairs, International Trade and Worship"), which answers to the [President](/wiki/President_of_Argentina "President of Argentina"). The country is one of the [G-15](/wiki/Group_of_15 "Group of 15") and [G-20 major economies](/wiki/G-20_major_economies "G-20 major economies") of the world, and a founding member of the [UN](/wiki/UN "UN"), [WBG](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group"), [WTO](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") and [OAS](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States").
In 2012 [Argentina was elected again](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_election,_2012 "United Nations Security Council election, 2012") to a two-year non-permanent position on the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") and is participating in major peacekeeping operations in [Haiti](/wiki/United_Nations_Stabilization_Mission_in_Haiti "United Nations Stabilization Mission in Haiti"), [Cyprus](/wiki/United_Nations_Peacekeeping_Force_in_Cyprus "United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus"), [Western Sahara](/wiki/Western_Sahara "Western Sahara") and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East").[[216]](#cite_note-231) Argentina is described as a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood198818Solomon19973-27)[[217]](#cite_note-Cooper-232)

A prominent Latin American[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuntington20006Nierop200161Lake200955Papadopoulos2010283Malamud20119Boughton2012101-28) and Southern Cone[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris198863AdlerGreve200978Ruiz-DanaGoldschagClaroBlanco200918-29) [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power"), Argentina cofounded [OEI](/wiki/Organization_of_Ibero-American_States "Organization of Ibero-American States") and [CELAC](/wiki/Community_of_Latin_American_and_Caribbean_States "Community of Latin American and Caribbean States").
It is also a founding member of the [Mercosur](/wiki/Mercosur "Mercosur") block, having Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") as partners. Since 2002 the country has emphasised its key role in [Latin American integration](/wiki/Latin_American_integration "Latin American integration"), and the block—which has some supranational legislative functions—is its first international priority.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalasso2011600vol._II-233)

Argentina claims 965,597 km2 (372,819 sq mi) in [Antarctica](/wiki/Argentine_Antarctica "Argentine Antarctica"), where it has the world's oldest [continuous state presence](/wiki/Orcadas_Base "Orcadas Base"), since 1904.[[219]](#cite_note-234) This overlaps claims by [Chile](/wiki/Chilean_Antarctic_Territory "Chilean Antarctic Territory") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/British_Antarctic_Territory "British Antarctic Territory"), though all such claims fall under the provisions of the 1961 [Antarctic Treaty](/wiki/Antarctic_Treaty "Antarctic Treaty"), of which Argentina is a founding signatory and permanent consulting member, with the [Antarctic Treaty Secretariat](/wiki/Antarctic_Treaty_Secretariat "Antarctic Treaty Secretariat") being based in Buenos Aires.[[220]](#cite_note-235)

Argentina [disputes sovereignty](/wiki/Falkland_Islands_sovereignty_dispute "Falkland Islands sovereignty dispute") over the British possessions of the Falkland Islands, [South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands](/wiki/South_Georgia_and_South_Sandwich_Islands_sovereignty_dispute "South Georgia and South Sandwich Islands sovereignty dispute").[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_ArgentinaT._P._1-236)[[222]](#cite_note-237) Argentina is a party to the [Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court](/wiki/Rome_Statute_of_the_International_Criminal_Court "Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court").[[223]](#cite_note-238) Argentina is a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") since 1998[[25]](#cite_note-Major_Non-NATO_Ally_Status-30) and an [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") candidate country since January 2022.[[224]](#cite_note-239)

### Military

Main article: [Armed Forces of the Argentine Republic](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Argentine_Republic "Armed Forces of the Argentine Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/A-4AR_Fightinghawk_2010.jpg/250px-A-4AR_Fightinghawk_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:A-4AR_Fightinghawk_2010.jpg)

[Lockheed Martin A-4AR Fightinghawk](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_A-4AR_Fightinghawk "Lockheed Martin A-4AR Fightinghawk") operated by the [Argentine Air Force](/wiki/Argentine_Air_Force "Argentine Air Force")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/D13_ARA_Sarandi_DN-SC-91-01826.jpg/250px-D13_ARA_Sarandi_DN-SC-91-01826.jpg)](/wiki/File:D13_ARA_Sarandi_DN-SC-91-01826.jpg)

Argentine destroyer [ARA *Sarandí* (D-13)](/wiki/ARA_Sarand%C3%AD_(D-13) "ARA Sarandí (D-13)")

The president holds the title of commander-in-chief of the Argentine Armed Forces, as part of a legal framework that imposes a strict separation between national defence and internal security systems:[[225]](#cite_note-240)[[226]](#cite_note-lsi-241) The [National Defence System](/wiki/Argentine_defense_industry "Argentine defense industry"), an exclusive responsibility of the federal government,[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._125–126-242) coordinated by the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defense_(Argentina) "Ministry of Defense (Argentina)"), and comprising the [Army](/wiki/Argentine_Army "Argentine Army"), the [Navy](/wiki/Argentine_Navy "Argentine Navy") and the [Air Force](/wiki/Argentine_Air_Force "Argentine Air Force").[[228]](#cite_note-243) Ruled and monitored by Congress[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._21,_75,_99-244) through the Houses' Defence Committees,[[230]](#cite_note-resdal-245) it is organised on the essential principle of legitimate self-defence: the repelling of any external military aggression in order to guarantee freedom of the people, national sovereignty, and territorial integrity.[[230]](#cite_note-resdal-245) Its secondary missions include committing to multinational operations within the framework of the United Nations, participating in internal support missions, assisting friendly countries, and establishing a sub-regional defence system.[[230]](#cite_note-resdal-245)

[Military service](/wiki/Military_service "Military service") is voluntary, with enlistment age between 18 and 24 years old and no [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription").[[231]](#cite_note-246) Argentina's defence has historically been one of the best equipped in the region, even managing [its own weapon research facilities, shipyards, ordnance, tank and plane factories](/wiki/Argentine_defense_industry "Argentine defense industry").[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaldifassiAbetti199465–86-247) However, real military expenditures declined steadily after the defeat in the [Falklands/Malvinas War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War") and the defence budget in 2011 was only about 0.74% of GDP, a historical minimum,[[233]](#cite_note-248) below the Latin American average. Within the defence budget itself, funding for training and even basic maintenance has been significantly cut, a factor contributing to the [accidental loss of the Argentine submarine San Juan](/wiki/Disappearance_of_ARA_San_Juan "Disappearance of ARA San Juan") in 2017. The result has been a steady erosion of Argentine military capabilities, with some arguing that Argentina had, by the end of the 2010s, ceased to be a capable military power.[[234]](#cite_note-249)

The [Interior Security System](/wiki/Interior_Security_System "Interior Security System") is jointly administered by the federal and subscribing provincial governments.[[226]](#cite_note-lsi-241) At the federal level it is coordinated by the Interior, [Security](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defense_(Argentina) "Ministry of Defense (Argentina)") and Justice ministries, and monitored by Congress.[[226]](#cite_note-lsi-241) It is enforced by the [Federal Police](/wiki/Argentine_Federal_Police "Argentine Federal Police"); the [Prefecture](/wiki/Argentine_Naval_Prefecture "Argentine Naval Prefecture"), which fulfills [coast guard](/wiki/Coast_guard "Coast guard") duties; the [Gendarmerie](/wiki/Argentine_National_Gendarmerie "Argentine National Gendarmerie"), which serves [border guard](/wiki/Border_guard "Border guard") tasks; and the [Airport Security Police](/wiki/Airport_Security_Police_(Argentina) "Airport Security Police (Argentina)").[[235]](#cite_note-250) At the provincial level it is coordinated by the respective internal security ministries and enforced by local police agencies.[[226]](#cite_note-lsi-241)

Argentina was the only South American country to send warships and cargo planes in 1991 to the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") under [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") mandate and has remained involved in [peacekeeping](/wiki/Peacekeeping "Peacekeeping") efforts in multiple locations such as [UNPROFOR](/wiki/UNPROFOR "UNPROFOR") in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia")/[Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"), [Gulf of Fonseca](/wiki/Gulf_of_Fonseca "Gulf of Fonseca"), [UNFICYP](/wiki/United_Nations_Peacekeeping_Force_in_Cyprus "United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus") in [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") (where among Army and Marines troops the Air Force provided the UN Air contingent since 1994) and [MINUSTAH](/wiki/MINUSTAH "MINUSTAH") in [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"). Argentina is the only Latin American country to maintain troops in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") during [SFOR](/wiki/SFOR "SFOR") (and later [EUFOR](/wiki/EUFOR "EUFOR")) operations where [combat engineers](/wiki/Combat_engineering "Combat engineering") of the Argentine Armed Forces are embedded in an [Italian brigade](/wiki/Italian_Army "Italian Army").

In 2007, an Argentine contingent including helicopters, boats and water purification plants was sent to help [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia") against their worst floods in decades.[[236]](#cite_note-251) In 2010 the Armed Forces were also involved in [Haiti](/wiki/Humanitarian_response_by_national_governments_to_the_2010_Haiti_earthquake "Humanitarian response by national governments to the 2010 Haiti earthquake") and [Chile](/wiki/Humanitarian_response_to_the_2010_Chile_earthquake "Humanitarian response to the 2010 Chile earthquake") humanitarian responses after their respective earthquakes.[[237]](#cite_note-252)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Argentina](/wiki/Economy_of_Argentina "Economy of Argentina")

See also: [Industry in Argentina](/wiki/Industry_in_Argentina "Industry in Argentina") and [Argentine foreign trade](/wiki/Argentine_foreign_trade "Argentine foreign trade")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Puerto_Madero_-_Puente_de_la_mujer_%2844673627614%29.jpg/250px-Puerto_Madero_-_Puente_de_la_mujer_%2844673627614%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Puerto_Madero_-_Puente_de_la_mujer_(44673627614).jpg)

The [Puerto Madero](/wiki/Puerto_Madero "Puerto Madero") business complex in [Buenos Aires CBD](/wiki/Buenos_Aires_Central_Business_District "Buenos Aires Central Business District")

Benefiting from rich [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resources "Natural resources"), a highly literate population, a diversified industrial base, and an export-oriented agricultural sector, Argentina's economy is Latin America's third-largest,[[238]](#cite_note-wsj1-253) and the second-largest in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America").[[239]](#cite_note-254) In 1913, Argentina was one of the wealthiest countries in the world by GDP per capita.[[240]](#cite_note-255) It has a "[very high](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index")" rating on the Human Development Index[[12]](#cite_note-UNHDR-14) and ranks 66th by [nominal GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita"),[[241]](#cite_note-256) with a considerable [internal market](/wiki/Single_market "Single market") size and a growing share of the [high-tech](/wiki/High-tech "High-tech") sector. As a [middle emerging economy](/wiki/Emerging_economy "Emerging economy") and one of the world's top developing nations, it is a member of the [G-20 major economies](/wiki/G-20_major_economies "G-20 major economies").[[242]](#cite_note-undp2013-257)[[O]](#cite_note-258)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Vi%C3%B1edos_de_Mendoza.jpg/250px-Vi%C3%B1edos_de_Mendoza.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vi%C3%B1edos_de_Mendoza.jpg)

A [vineyard](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard") in [Mendoza](/wiki/Mendoza_Province "Mendoza Province"). Argentina is the [sixth-largest producer of wine](/wiki/List_of_wine-producing_regions "List of wine-producing regions").[[243]](#cite_note-Johnson_atlas_pg_300-301-259)

Argentina is the largest producer in the world of [yerba mate](/wiki/Yerba_mate "Yerba mate") (due to the large domestic consumption of [mate](/wiki/Mate_(drink) "Mate (drink)")), one of the five largest producers in the world of [soybeans](/wiki/Soybeans "Soybeans"), [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [sunflower seed](/wiki/Sunflower_seed "Sunflower seed"), [lemon](/wiki/Lemon "Lemon") and [pear](/wiki/Pear "Pear"), one of the ten largest producers in the world of [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley"), [grape](/wiki/Grape "Grape"), [artichoke](/wiki/Artichoke "Artichoke"), [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco") and [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton"), and one of the 15 largest producers in the world of [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane"), [sorghum](/wiki/Sorghum "Sorghum") and [grapefruit](/wiki/Grapefruit "Grapefruit"). It is the largest producer in South America of wheat, sunflower seed, barley, lemon and pear.[[244]](#cite_note-260)[[245]](#cite_note-261) In [wine](/wiki/Wine "Wine"), Argentina is usually among the ten [largest producers in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_wine_production "List of countries by wine production").[[246]](#cite_note-262) Argentina is a traditional meat exporter, having been, in 2019, the 4th world producer of [beef](/wiki/Beef "Beef"), with a production of 3 million tons (only behind US, Brazil and China), the 4th world producer of [honey](/wiki/Honey "Honey"), and the 10th world producer of [wool](/wiki/Wool "Wool"), in addition to other relevant productions.[[247]](#cite_note-263)[[248]](#cite_note-264)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Producci%C3%B3n_Minera_en_la_Cordillera_de_los_Andes%2C_prov._de_San_Juan.jpg/250px-Producci%C3%B3n_Minera_en_la_Cordillera_de_los_Andes%2C_prov._de_San_Juan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Producci%C3%B3n_Minera_en_la_Cordillera_de_los_Andes,_prov._de_San_Juan.jpg)

[Veladero mine](/wiki/Veladero_mine "Veladero mine") is a [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold") mine in [San Juan Province](/wiki/San_Juan_Province,_Argentina "San Juan Province, Argentina").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Fiat-C%C3%B3rdoba.jpg/250px-Fiat-C%C3%B3rdoba.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fiat-C%C3%B3rdoba.jpg)

[Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") factory in [Córdoba, Argentina](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Argentina "Córdoba, Argentina")

The [mining industry](/wiki/Mining_industry "Mining industry") of Argentina is less prominent compared to other countries but stands out as the fourth-largest producer of [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium"),[[249]](#cite_note-265) the 11th-largest of [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver")[[250]](#cite_note-266) and 17th-largest of [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold")[[251]](#cite_note-267) worldwide. Argentina excels in [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") production, being the largest producer in South America and the 18th-largest globally. Argentina produces an average of 500,000 barrels/day of [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), despite the under-utilisation of the Vaca Muerta field due to technical and financial limitations in resource extraction.[[252]](#cite_note-268)[[253]](#cite_note-269)

In 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Argentina&action=edit), [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing") accounted for 20.3% of GDP—the largest sector in Argentina's economy.[[254]](#cite_note-infoeco1-270) Well-integrated into Argentine agriculture, half of the industrial exports have rural origin.[[254]](#cite_note-infoeco1-270) With a 6.5% production growth rate in 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Argentina&action=edit),[[255]](#cite_note-271) the diversified manufacturing sector rests on a steadily growing network of [industrial parks](/wiki/Industrial_park "Industrial park"), 314 in 2013.[[256]](#cite_note-272)[[257]](#cite_note-273) In 2012, the leading sectors by volume were: food processing, beverages and tobacco products; motor vehicles and auto parts; [textiles](/wiki/Textiles "Textiles") and leather; [refinery products](/wiki/Petroleum_refineries "Petroleum refineries") and [biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel"); chemicals and pharmaceuticals; [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel"), [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium") and [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"); industrial and farm machinery; home appliances and furniture; plastics and tires; glass and cement; and recording and print media.[[254]](#cite_note-infoeco1-270) Argentina has since long been one of the top five wine-producing countries in the world.[[254]](#cite_note-infoeco1-270)

Argentina ranks 85th out of 180 countries in the [Transparency International](/wiki/Transparency_International "Transparency International")'s 2017 [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index"),[[258]](#cite_note-274) an improvement of 22 positions over its 2014 rankings.[[259]](#cite_note-275) Argentina settled its long-standing debt default crisis in 2016 with the so-called [vulture funds](/wiki/Vulture_funds "Vulture funds") after the election of Mauricio Macri, allowing Argentina to enter capital markets for the first time in a decade.[[260]](#cite_note-276) The government of Argentina defaulted in May 2020 by failing to pay a $500 million bill by its due date to its creditors. Negotiations for the restructuring of $66 billion of its debt continue.[[261]](#cite_note-277)

High [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation")—a weakness of the Argentine economy for decades—has become a trouble once again,[[262]](#cite_note-nyt-2011-02-06-278) recently peaking at 220% in 2024.[[263]](#cite_note-Argentina:_Inflation_rate_from_2004_to_2030-279) Approximately 43% of the Argentina's population lived below the poverty line in 2023.[[264]](#cite_note-280) To deter it and support the peso, the government imposed foreign currency control.[[265]](#cite_note-281) [Income distribution](/wiki/Income_distribution "Income distribution"), having improved since 2002, is classified as "medium", although it is still considerably unequal.[[11]](#cite_note-gini-13)

On 10 December 2023, [Javier Milei](/wiki/Javier_Milei "Javier Milei") was sworn in as president. He pursued deregulation policies to alleviate the national economic crisis.[[266]](#cite_note-282)[[267]](#cite_note-283) In January 2024, Argentina's poverty rate reached 57.4%, the highest poverty rate in the country since 2004.[[268]](#cite_note-284) By the second semester of 2024, however, poverty levels had fallen sharply to 38%, the lowest since 2022.[[269]](#cite_note-285) In November 2024, Argentina's monthly inflation rate slowed to 2.4%, the lowest in over four years. Annual inflation was expected to end 2024 closer to 100%.[[270]](#cite_note-286) Favourable results and normalisation in Argentina's economy are expected to continue in 2025. The annual inflation rate is expected to be below 30% in 2025. Economic activity has also begun to recover after the severe recession at the beginning of 2024. The economy is expected to expand by more than 4% in 2025.[[271]](#cite_note-287)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Argentina](/wiki/Tourism_in_Argentina "Tourism in Argentina")

Argentina had 5.57 million visitors in 2013, the top international tourist destination in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), and the second in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") after Mexico.[[272]](#cite_note-UNWTO2014-288) Revenues from international tourists reached US$4.41 billion in 2013, down from US$4.89 billion in 2012.[[272]](#cite_note-UNWTO2014-288) Argentina's capital city, [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"), is the most visited city in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America").[[273]](#cite_note-289) There are 30 [National Parks of Argentina](/wiki/National_Parks_of_Argentina "National Parks of Argentina") including many [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_in_Argentina "World Heritage Sites in Argentina").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Panorama_from_Cerro_Campanario_-_Bariloche_-_panoramio.jpg/1920px-Panorama_from_Cerro_Campanario_-_Bariloche_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panorama_from_Cerro_Campanario_-_Bariloche_-_panoramio.jpg "A panorama of the Nahuel Huapi National Park and the Nahuel Huapi Lake from Cerro Campanario, Bariloche")

A panorama of the [Nahuel Huapi National Park](/wiki/Nahuel_Huapi_National_Park "Nahuel Huapi National Park") and the [Nahuel Huapi Lake](/wiki/Nahuel_Huapi_Lake "Nahuel Huapi Lake") from Cerro Campanario, [Bariloche](/wiki/Bariloche "Bariloche")

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Argentina](/wiki/Transport_in_Argentina "Transport in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Autopista_Rosario_-_C%C3%B3rdoba_km_365_hacia_el_Este.JPG/250px-Autopista_Rosario_-_C%C3%B3rdoba_km_365_hacia_el_Este.JPG)](/wiki/File:Autopista_Rosario_-_C%C3%B3rdoba_km_365_hacia_el_Este.JPG)

A stretch of [National Route 9](/wiki/National_Route_9_(Argentina) "National Route 9 (Argentina)") between [Rosario](/wiki/Rosario,_Santa_Fe "Rosario, Santa Fe") and [Córdoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Argentina "Córdoba, Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Mar_del_plata_station_1.jpg/250px-Mar_del_plata_station_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mar_del_plata_station_1.jpg)

A [Trenes Argentinos](/wiki/Trenes_Argentinos "Trenes Argentinos") [CNR CKD8G](/wiki/CNR_CKD8G "CNR CKD8G") at [Mar del Plata railway station](/wiki/Mar_del_Plata_railway_and_bus_station "Mar del Plata railway and bus station")

In 2004, Buenos Aires, all provincial capitals except Ushuaia, and all medium-sized towns were interconnected by 69,412 km (43,131 mi) of paved roads, out of a road network of 231,374 km (143,769 mi).[[274]](#cite_note-290) In 2021, Argentina had about 2,800 km (1,740 mi) of [dual carriageways](/wiki/Dual_carriageway "Dual carriageway"), most leaving the capital [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"), linking it with cities such as [Rosario](/wiki/Rosario,_Santa_Fe "Rosario, Santa Fe") and [Córdoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba_(Argentina) "Córdoba (Argentina)"), [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe_(Argentina) "Santa Fe (Argentina)"), [Mar del Plata](/wiki/Mar_del_Plata "Mar del Plata") and [Paso de los Libres](/wiki/Paso_de_los_Libres "Paso de los Libres") (bordering Brazil). There are also [dual carriageways](/wiki/Dual_carriageway "Dual carriageway") leaving from [Mendoza](/wiki/Mendoza,_Argentina "Mendoza, Argentina") towards the capital, and between Córdoba and Santa Fé, among other locations.[[275]](#cite_note-291) Nevertheless, this road infrastructure is still inadequate and cannot handle the sharply growing demand caused by deterioration of the railway system.[[276]](#cite_note-eotn1-292)

Argentina has the largest [railway system](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Argentina "Rail transport in Argentina") in Latin America, with 36,966 km (22,970 mi) of operating lines in 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Argentina&action=edit), out of a network of almost 48,000 km (29,826 mi).[[277]](#cite_note-293) This system links all 23 provinces, plus Buenos Aires City, and connects with all neighbouring countries.[[276]](#cite_note-eotn1-292) There are four incompatible [gauges](/wiki/Track_gauge "Track gauge") in use; this forces virtually all interregional freight traffic to pass through Buenos Aires.[[276]](#cite_note-eotn1-292) The system has been in decline since the 1940s: regularly running up large budgetary deficits. By 1991, it was transporting 1,400 times less goods than it did in 1973.[[276]](#cite_note-eotn1-292)

However, in recent years the system has experienced a [greater degree of investment](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Argentina#Recent_developments_and_moves_towards_re-nationalisation "Rail transport in Argentina") from the state, in both commuter rail lines and long-distance lines, renewing rolling stock and infrastructure.[[278]](#cite_note-294)[[279]](#cite_note-295) In April 2015, by overwhelming majority the [Argentine Senate](/wiki/Argentine_Senate "Argentine Senate") passed a law which recreated [Ferrocarriles Argentinos](/wiki/Ferrocarriles_Argentinos "Ferrocarriles Argentinos"), effectively renationalising the country's railways, a move which saw support from all major political parties on both sides of the political spectrum.[[280]](#cite_note-296)[[281]](#cite_note-297)[[282]](#cite_note-298)

In 2012, there were about 11,000 km (6,835 mi) of [waterways](/wiki/Waterway "Waterway"),[[283]](#cite_note-299) mostly comprising the La Plata, Paraná, Paraguay and Uruguay rivers, with Buenos Aires, [Zárate](/wiki/Z%C3%A1rate,_Buenos_Aires "Zárate, Buenos Aires"), [Campana](/wiki/Campana,_Buenos_Aires "Campana, Buenos Aires"), Rosario, San Lorenzo, Santa Fe, [Barranqueras](/wiki/Barranqueras "Barranqueras") and San Nicolas de los Arroyos as the main [fluvial ports](/wiki/Fluvial_port "Fluvial port").
Some of the largest [sea ports](/wiki/Sea_port "Sea port") are [La Plata](/wiki/La_Plata "La Plata")–[Ensenada](/wiki/Ensenada,_Buenos_Aires "Ensenada, Buenos Aires"), Bahía Blanca, [Mar del Plata](/wiki/Mar_del_Plata "Mar del Plata"), [Quequén](/wiki/Quequ%C3%A9n "Quequén")–[Necochea](/wiki/Necochea "Necochea"), [Comodoro Rivadavia](/wiki/Comodoro_Rivadavia "Comodoro Rivadavia"), [Puerto Deseado](/wiki/Puerto_Deseado "Puerto Deseado"), [Puerto Madryn](/wiki/Puerto_Madryn "Puerto Madryn"), Ushuaia and [San Antonio Oeste](/wiki/San_Antonio_Oeste "San Antonio Oeste").
Buenos Aires has historically been the most important port. Since the 1990s the Up-River port region has become dominant: stretching along 67 km (42 mi) of the Paraná river shore in Santa Fe province, it includes 17 ports and in 2013 accounted for 50% of all exports.

In 2013, there were 161 airports with paved runways[[284]](#cite_note-300) out of more than a thousand.[[276]](#cite_note-eotn1-292) The [Ezeiza International Airport](/wiki/Ezeiza_International_Airport "Ezeiza International Airport"), about 35 km (22 mi) from downtown Buenos Aires,[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAeberhardBensonPhillips200076-301) is the largest in Argentina, followed by [Cataratas del Iguazú](/wiki/Cataratas_del_Iguaz%C3%BA_International_Airport "Cataratas del Iguazú International Airport") in Misiones, and [El Plumerillo](/wiki/El_Plumerillo_International_Airport "El Plumerillo International Airport") in Mendoza.[[276]](#cite_note-eotn1-292) [Aeroparque](/wiki/Aeroparque "Aeroparque"), in the city of Buenos Aires, is the most important domestic airport.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAeberhardBensonPhillips200024–25-302)

### Energy

Main article: [Electricity sector in Argentina](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_Argentina "Electricity sector in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Central_Nuclear_Atucha_I_-_II.JPG/250px-Central_Nuclear_Atucha_I_-_II.JPG)](/wiki/File:Central_Nuclear_Atucha_I_-_II.JPG)

[Atucha Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Atucha_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Atucha Nuclear Power Plant") was the first nuclear power plant in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").[[287]](#cite_note-303)

In 2020, more than 60% of Argentina's electricity came from non-renewable sources such as natural gas, oil and coal. 27% came from [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower"), 7.3% from wind and solar energy and 4.4% from nuclear energy.[[288]](#cite_note-304) At the end of 2021 Argentina was the 21st country in the world in terms of installed hydroelectric power (11.3 GW), the 26th country in the world in terms of installed wind energy (3.2 GW) and the 43rd country in the world in terms of installed solar energy (1.0 GW).[[289]](#cite_note-305)

The wind potential of the Patagonia region is considered gigantic, with estimates that the area could provide enough electricity to sustain the consumption of a country like Brazil alone. However, Argentina has faced infrastructural challenges in transferring electricity from wind-rich rural areas to its population hubs.[[290]](#cite_note-306)

In 1974, Argentina was the first country in Latin America to put a commercial nuclear power plant online, [Atucha I](/wiki/Atucha_I_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Atucha I Nuclear Power Plant"). Although the Argentine-built parts for that station amounted to 10% of the total, the nuclear fuel it uses are from Argentina. Later nuclear power stations employed a higher percentage of Argentine-built components. [Embalse](/wiki/Embalse_Nuclear_Power_Station "Embalse Nuclear Power Station"), finished in 1983, used 30% and the 2011 [Atucha II](/wiki/Atucha_II_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Atucha II Nuclear Power Plant") reactor used 40%.[[291]](#cite_note-307)

### Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Argentina](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Argentina "Science and technology in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Leloir_festejando.jpg/250px-Leloir_festejando.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leloir_festejando.jpg)

[Luis Federico Leloir](/wiki/Luis_Federico_Leloir "Luis Federico Leloir") (left) and his staff toast his 1970 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry").

Argentines have received three [Nobel Prizes](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") in the Sciences. [Bernardo Houssay](/wiki/Bernardo_Houssay "Bernardo Houssay"), the first Latin American recipient, discovered the role of [pituitary hormones](/wiki/Pituitary_gland "Pituitary gland") in regulating [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose") in animals, and shared the [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine") in 1947. [Luis Leloir](/wiki/Luis_Leloir "Luis Leloir") discovered how organisms store energy converting glucose into [glycogen](/wiki/Glycogen "Glycogen") and the compounds which are fundamental in [metabolising](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") [carbohydrates](/wiki/Carbohydrate "Carbohydrate"), receiving the [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") in 1970. [César Milstein](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Milstein "César Milstein") did extensive research in [antibodies](/wiki/Antibody "Antibody"), sharing the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1984. Argentine research has led to treatments for [heart diseases](/wiki/Heart_disease "Heart disease") and several forms of cancer. [Domingo Liotta](/wiki/Domingo_Liotta "Domingo Liotta") designed and developed the first [artificial heart](/wiki/Artificial_heart "Artificial heart") that was successfully implanted in a human being in 1969. [René Favaloro](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Favaloro "René Favaloro") developed the techniques and performed the world's first [coronary bypass surgery](/wiki/Coronary_artery_bypass_surgery "Coronary artery bypass surgery").

Argentina was the first country in Latin America to design and build a [research reactor](/wiki/Research_reactor "Research reactor") with homegrown technology, the [RA-1 Enrico Fermi](/wiki/RA-1_Enrico_Fermi "RA-1 Enrico Fermi"). This reliance on the development of its own nuclear-related technologies, instead of buying them abroad, was a constant of Argentina's nuclear programme conducted by the civilian [National Atomic Energy Commission](/wiki/National_Atomic_Energy_Commission "National Atomic Energy Commission") (CNEA). Nuclear facilities with Argentine technology have been built in Peru, Algeria, Australia and Egypt. In 1983, Argentina admitted having the capability of producing weapon-grade [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium"), a major step needed to assemble [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon"). Since then, Argentina has pledged to use nuclear power only for peaceful purposes.[[292]](#cite_note-308) As a member of the Board of Governors of the [International Atomic Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Atomic_Energy_Agency "International Atomic Energy Agency"), Argentina supports nuclear non-proliferation efforts[[293]](#cite_note-309) and is committed to global nuclear security.[[294]](#cite_note-310)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/SAOCOM_1A_-_sala_de_integraci%C3%B3n_-_CEATSA_-_072018_-22_0.jpg/250px-SAOCOM_1A_-_sala_de_integraci%C3%B3n_-_CEATSA_-_072018_-22_0.jpg)](/wiki/File:SAOCOM_1A_-_sala_de_integraci%C3%B3n_-_CEATSA_-_072018_-22_0.jpg)

The [SAOCOM 1A](/wiki/SAOCOM_1A "SAOCOM 1A") inside the facilities of [CEATSA](/wiki/CEATSA "CEATSA")

Despite its modest budget and numerous setbacks, academics and the sciences in Argentina have enjoyed international respect since the 1900s, when [Luis Agote](/wiki/Luis_Agote "Luis Agote") devised the first safe and effective means of [blood transfusion](/wiki/Blood_transfusion "Blood transfusion") as well as [René Favaloro](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Favaloro "René Favaloro"), who was a pioneer in the improvement of the [coronary artery bypass surgery](/wiki/Coronary_artery_bypass_surgery "Coronary artery bypass surgery"). Argentine scientists are still on the cutting edge in fields such as [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology"), [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [computer sciences](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), molecular biology, oncology, ecology and cardiology. [Juan Maldacena](/wiki/Juan_Maldacena "Juan Maldacena"), an Argentine-American scientist, is a leading figure in [string theory](/wiki/String_theory "String theory").

Space research has become increasingly active in Argentina. Argentine-built satellites include [LUSAT-1](/w/index.php?title=LUSAT-1&action=edit&redlink=1 "LUSAT-1 (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lusat_1 "es:Lusat 1")] (1990), [Víctor-1](/w/index.php?title=V%C3%ADctor-1&action=edit&redlink=1 "Víctor-1 (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/V%C3%ADctor-1 "es:Víctor-1")] (1996), [PEHUENSAT-1](/wiki/PEHUENSAT-1 "PEHUENSAT-1") (2007),[[295]](#cite_note-311) and those developed by [CONAE](/wiki/CONAE "CONAE"), the Argentine space agency, of the SAC series.[[296]](#cite_note-312) Argentina has its own satellite programme, nuclear power station designs (4th generation) and public nuclear energy company [INVAP](/wiki/INVAP "INVAP"), which provides several countries with nuclear reactors.[[297]](#cite_note-science-313) Established in 1991, the [CONAE](/wiki/CONAE "CONAE") has since launched two satellites successfully.[[298]](#cite_note-314)

In June 2009, CONAE secured an agreement with the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") for the installation of a 35-m diametre antenna and other mission support facilities at the [Pierre Auger Observatory](/wiki/Pierre_Auger_Observatory "Pierre Auger Observatory"), the world's foremost [cosmic ray](/wiki/Cosmic_ray "Cosmic ray") observatory.[[299]](#cite_note-315) The facility will contribute to numerous ESA space probes, as well as CONAE's own, domestic research projects. Chosen from 20 potential sites and one of only three such ESA installations in the world, the new antenna will create a triangulation which will allow the ESA to ensure mission coverage around the clock.[[300]](#cite_note-316) Argentina was ranked 76th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2024,[[301]](#cite_note-317) and was ranked 77th in 2025.[[302]](#cite_note-318)

### Argentine Society for the Study of Mammals

The Sociedad Argentina para el Estudio de los Mamíferos, SAREM (Argentine Society for the Study of Mammals) in [Mendoza](/wiki/Mendoza,_Argentina "Mendoza, Argentina"), in collaboration with the Center for Science and Technology, Mendoza (CCT), the [National Council of Science and Technology](/wiki/National_Council_of_Science_and_Technology "National Council of Science and Technology") (CONICET), and the [International Federation of Mammalogists](/wiki/International_Union_of_Biological_Sciences#International_Federation_of_Mammalogists "International Union of Biological Sciences") (IFM), hosted the 2009 International Mammalogical Congresses in Mendoza. The Latin American Mammalogy Network (Red Latinoamericana de Mastozoología, or RELAM) was created during the 2009 IMC Congress, cofounded by SAREM and the Asociación Boliviana de Investigadores en Mamíferos (Bolivian Association of Mammal Researchers, ABIMA).[[303]](#cite_note-319) The electronic scientific journal *Therya*, conceived during IMC 10, began publication by the Mexican Association of Mammalogy (AMMAC), in 2010. Doctor William Z. Lidicker Jr., who presided over IMC 10, was the editor.[[304]](#cite_note-320)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Argentina](/wiki/Demographics_of_Argentina "Demographics of Argentina") and [Argentines](/wiki/Argentines "Argentines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Population_density_by_municipality_in_Argentina%2C_2022.svg/250px-Population_density_by_municipality_in_Argentina%2C_2022.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Population_density_by_municipality_in_Argentina,_2022.svg)

A map of Argentina's population density, 2022

The 2022 [census](/wiki/Census "Census") [[INDEC](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Statistics_and_Census_of_Argentina "National Institute of Statistics and Census of Argentina")] counted 46,044,703 inhabitants, up from 40,117,096 in 2010.[[9]](#cite_note-P12-CENSO-11)[[305]](#cite_note-321)[[306]](#cite_note-322) The population in 2024 is estimated to have increased to 47,067,441 inhabitants.[[307]](#cite_note-Proyecciones_y_estimaciones-323) Argentina ranks third in South America in total population, fourth in Latin America and 33rd globally. Its population density of 15 persons per square kilometre of land area is well below the world average of 50 persons. The population growth rate in 2010 was an estimated 1.03% annually, with a birth rate of 17.7 live births per 1,000 inhabitants and a mortality rate of 7.4 deaths per 1,000 inhabitants. Since 2010, the crude [net migration rate](/wiki/Net_migration_rate "Net migration rate") has ranged from below zero to up to four immigrants per 1,000 inhabitants per year.[[308]](#cite_note-324)

Argentina is in the midst of a [demographic transition](/wiki/Demographic_transition "Demographic transition") to an older and slower-growing population. The proportion of people under 15 is 25.6%, a little below the world average of 28%, and the proportion of people 65 and older is relatively high at 10.8%. In Latin America, this is second only to [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") and well above the world average, which is currently 7%. Argentina has a comparatively low [infant mortality rate](/wiki/Infant_mortality_rate "Infant mortality rate"). Argentina's 2010 birth rate of 2.3 children per woman was considerably below the high of 7.0 children born per woman in 1895,[[309]](#cite_note-325) though still nearly twice as high as in Spain or Italy, which are culturally and demographically similar.[[310]](#cite_note-326)[[311]](#cite_note-327) In 2015, the median age was 31.9 years and [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") at birth was 77.14 years.[[312]](#cite_note-328)

Attitudes towards [LGBT people are generally positive](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Argentina "LGBT rights in Argentina") within Argentina.[[313]](#cite_note-329) In 2010, Argentina became the first country in Latin America, the second in the Americas, and the tenth worldwide to legalise [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage").[[314]](#cite_note-330)[[315]](#cite_note-331)

### Urbanisation

See also: [List of cities in Argentina by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Argentina_by_population "List of cities in Argentina by population") and [List of cities in Argentina](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Argentina "List of cities in Argentina")

Argentina is highly urbanised, with 92% of its population living in cities:[[316]](#cite_note-332) the ten largest metropolitan areas account for half of the population.
About 3 million people live in the city of Buenos Aires. The Greater Buenos Aires metropolitan area has around 13 million, making it one of the largest urban areas in the world.[[317]](#cite_note-majorcities-333) The metropolitan areas of Córdoba and Rosario have around 1.3 million inhabitants each.[[317]](#cite_note-majorcities-333) Mendoza, San Miguel de Tucumán, La Plata, Mar del Plata, Salta and Santa Fe have at least half a million people each.[[317]](#cite_note-majorcities-333)

The population is unequally distributed: about 60% live in the Pampas region (21% of the total area), including 15 million people in Buenos Aires province. The provinces of Córdoba and Santa Fe, and the city of Buenos Aires have 3 million each. Seven other provinces have over one million people each: Mendoza, Tucumán, Entre Ríos, Salta, Chaco, Corrientes and Misiones. With 64.3 inhabitants per square kilometre (167/sq mi), Tucumán is the only Argentine province more densely populated than the world average. By contrast, the southern province of Santa Cruz has around 1.1/km2 (2.8/sq mi).[[318]](#cite_note-334)
