### Ethnography

Main article: [Argentines](/wiki/Argentines "Argentines")

See also: [Ethnography of Argentina](/wiki/Ethnography_of_Argentina "Ethnography of Argentina") and [Immigration to Argentina](/wiki/Immigration_to_Argentina "Immigration to Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Map_showing_the_ethnic_groups_in_Argentina.png/250px-Map_showing_the_ethnic_groups_in_Argentina.png)](/wiki/File:Map_showing_the_ethnic_groups_in_Argentina.png)

Proportion of European and indigenous peoples in Argentina

Argentina is considered a country of immigrants.[[320]](#cite_note-encuesta-336)[[321]](#cite_note-Coke-337)[[322]](#cite_note-338) Argentines usually refer to the country as a *crisol de razas* (crucible of races, or [melting pot](/wiki/Melting_pot "Melting pot")). A 2010 study conducted on 218 individuals by the Argentine geneticist [Daniel Corach](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Corach&action=edit&redlink=1 "Daniel Corach (page does not exist)") established that the average genetic ancestry of Argentines is 79% European (mainly Italian and Spanish), 18% indigenous and 4.3% African. 63.6% of the tested group had at least one ancestor [who was Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Argentina "Indigenous peoples in Argentina").[[323]](#cite_note-339)[[324]](#cite_note-340) The majority of Argentines descend from multiple European ethnic groups, primarily of [Italian](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people") and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_people "Spanish people") descent, with over 25 million Argentines, almost 60% of the population, having some partial Italian origins.[[325]](#cite_note-341)

Argentina is home to a notable [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Argentine "Asian Argentine") population, the majority of whom are descended from either West Asians, namely [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_people "Lebanese people") and [Syrians](/wiki/Syrians "Syrians"),[[326]](#cite_note-Lizcano2007-342) or East Asians, such as the [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people"),[[327]](#cite_note-343) [Koreans](/wiki/Korean_people "Korean people"), and the [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_people "Japanese people").[[328]](#cite_note-344) The latter of whom number around 180,000 individuals. The total number of [Arab Argentines](/wiki/Arab_Argentines "Arab Argentines") (most of whom are of Lebanese or Syrian origin) is estimated to be 1.3 to 3.5 million. Many immigrated from Asian countries to Argentina during the latter half of 19th century, and the first half of the 20th century.[[329]](#cite_note-Elindependiente-345)[[330]](#cite_note-346) Most Arab Argentines belong to the Catholic Church, including both the Latin Church and the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), or the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"). A minority are [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"). In 2018, there were 180,000 [Alawites](/wiki/Alawites "Alawites") in Argentina.[[331]](#cite_note-347)[[332]](#cite_note-348)

Since the 1970s, immigration has mostly been coming from [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"), [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay") and [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), with smaller numbers from the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"), Ecuador and [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania").[[333]](#cite_note-349) In 2008, the Argentine government estimated that 750,000 inhabitants lack official documents and has launched a programme[[334]](#cite_note-350) to encourage illegal immigrants to declare their status in return for two-year residence visas—so far over 670,000 applications have been processed under the programme.[[335]](#cite_note-351) As of July 2023, more than 18,500 Russians have come to Argentina after the [Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[336]](#cite_note-352)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Argentina](/wiki/Languages_of_Argentina "Languages of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Dialectos_del_idioma_espa%C3%B1ol_en_Argentina.png/250px-Dialectos_del_idioma_espa%C3%B1ol_en_Argentina.png)](/wiki/File:Dialectos_del_idioma_espa%C3%B1ol_en_Argentina.png)

Dialectal variants of the [Spanish language](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") in Argentina

The *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")*[[P]](#cite_note-353) official language is [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), spoken by almost all Argentines.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354)
Argentina is the largest [Spanish-speaking society](/wiki/Hispanophone "Hispanophone") that universally employs *[voseo](/wiki/Voseo "Voseo")*, the use of the [pronoun](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun") *vos* instead of *tú* ("you"), which imposes the use of alternative verb forms as well.

Owing to the extensive Argentine geography, Spanish has a strong variation among regions, although the prevalent dialect is *[Rioplatense](/wiki/Rioplatense_Spanish "Rioplatense Spanish")*, primarily spoken in the Pampean and Patagonian regions and accented similarly to the [Neapolitan language](/wiki/Neapolitan_language "Neapolitan language").[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColantoniGurlekian2004107–119-355) Italian and other European immigrants influenced *[Lunfardo](/wiki/Lunfardo "Lunfardo")*—the regional slang—permeating the vernacular vocabulary of other Latin American countries as well.

There are several second languages in widespread use among the Argentine population: [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") (by 2.8 million people);[[339]](#cite_note-356) [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") (by 1.5 million people);[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354)[[Q]](#cite_note-357) [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") (specifically its [Northern Levantine dialect](/wiki/Levantine_Arabic "Levantine Arabic"), by one million people);[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354) [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") (by 200,000 people);[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354)[[R]](#cite_note-358) [Guaraní](/wiki/Guarani_language "Guarani language") (by 200,000 people,[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354) mostly in Corrientes and Misiones);[[2]](#cite_note-Corrientes-5598-3) [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") (by 174,000 people);[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354) [Quechua](/wiki/Quechua_language "Quechua language") (by 65,000 people, mostly in the Northwest);[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354) [Wichí](/wiki/Wich%C3%AD_languages "Wichí languages") (by 53,700 people, mainly in Chaco[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354) where, along with [Kom](/wiki/Kom_language_(South_America) "Kom language (South America)") and [Moqoit](/wiki/Moqoit_language "Moqoit language"), it is official *de jure*);[[340]](#cite_note-kom-359) [Vlax Romani](/wiki/Vlax_Romani_language "Vlax Romani language") (by 52,000 people);[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354) [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language") (by [40,000 people](/wiki/Albanians_in_South_America "Albanians in South America"));[[341]](#cite_note-360) [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language") (by 32,000 people);[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354) [Aymara](/wiki/Aymara_language "Aymara language") (by 30,000 people, mostly in the Northwest);[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354) and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") (by 27,000 people).[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisSimonsFennig2014-354)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Argentina](/wiki/Religion_in_Argentina "Religion in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Francis_2013.jpg/250px-Francis_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francis_2013.jpg)

[Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis"), the first pope from the Americas, was born and raised in Argentina.

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is the largest religion in Argentina. The Constitution guarantees [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion").[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._14,_20-361) Although it enforces neither an official nor a state faith,[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFayt1985347Bidart_Campos200553-362) it gives [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") a preferential status.[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._2-363)[[S]](#cite_note-365)

In 2008, Argentines were 76.5% [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), 11.3% [Agnostics](/wiki/Agnostic "Agnostic") and [Atheists](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist"), 9% [Evangelical Protestants](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"), 1.2% [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), and 0.9% [Mormons](/wiki/Mormon "Mormon"), while 1.2% followed other religions, including [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism").[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMallimaciEsquivelIrrazábal20089-366) These figures appear to have changed quite significantly in recent years: in 2017, Catholics were 66% of the population, indicating a drop of 10.5% in nine years, and the nonreligious in the country standing at 21% of the population, indicating an almost doubling over the same period.[[347]](#cite_note-Latinobarometro_2017-367) In 2024, Catholics were 63% of the population. 20% of Argentines are irreligious, 9% are Protestants, and 8% follow another religion or didn't answer.[[348]](#cite_note-368)

Argentina is home to both one of the [largest Muslim](/wiki/Islam_in_Argentina "Islam in Argentina")[[345]](#cite_note-irfr1-364) and [largest Jewish](/wiki/Jewish_Argentine "Jewish Argentine") communities in Latin America, the latter being the seventh most populous in the world.[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDellaPergola201350-369) Argentina is a member of the [International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance](/wiki/International_Holocaust_Remembrance_Alliance "International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance").[[345]](#cite_note-irfr1-364)

Argentines show high individualisation and deinstitutionalisation of religious beliefs.[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMallimaciEsquivelIrrazábal200821-370) 23.8% claim to always attend religious services; 49.1% seldom do and 26.8% never do.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMallimaciEsquivelIrrazábal200824-371)

On 13 March 2013, Argentine [Jorge Mario Bergoglio](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis"), the [Cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)") [Archbishop of Buenos Aires](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Buenos_Aires "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Buenos Aires"), was [elected](/wiki/Papal_conclave,_2013 "Papal conclave, 2013") [Bishop of Rome](/wiki/Pope "Pope") and [Supreme Pontiff](/wiki/Supreme_Pontiff "Supreme Pontiff") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). He took the name "[Francis](/wiki/St._Francis_of_Assisi "St. Francis of Assisi")", and he became the first Pope from either the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") or from the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"). He is the first Pope born outside of Europe since the [election](/wiki/Papal_conclave "Papal conclave") of [Pope Gregory III](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_III "Pope Gregory III"), who was [Syrian](/wiki/Syrian "Syrian"), in 741.[[352]](#cite_note-372)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Argentina](/wiki/Education_in_Argentina "Education in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/School_of_Law_-_University_of_Buenos_Aires_%284729499370%29.jpg/250px-School_of_Law_-_University_of_Buenos_Aires_%284729499370%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:School_of_Law_-_University_of_Buenos_Aires_(4729499370).jpg)

[Faculty of Law](/wiki/Faculty_of_Law,_University_of_Buenos_Aires "Faculty of Law, University of Buenos Aires") of the [University of Buenos Aires](/wiki/University_of_Buenos_Aires "University of Buenos Aires")

The Argentine education system consists of four levels.[[353]](#cite_note-373) An initial level for children between 45 days to 5 years old, with the last two years[[354]](#cite_note-374) being compulsory. An elementary or [lower school](/wiki/Lower_school "Lower school") mandatory level lasting 6 or 7 years.[[T]](#cite_note-leveldiff-375) In 2010, the [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_rate "Literacy rate") was 98.07%.[[355]](#cite_note-educ1-376) A secondary or [high school](/wiki/High_school "High school") mandatory level lasts 5 or 6 years.[[T]](#cite_note-leveldiff-375) In 2010, 38.5% of people over age 20 had completed secondary school.[[356]](#cite_note-educ2-377) Higher education is divided into tertiary, university and post-graduate sub-levels. In 2013, there were 47 [national public universities](/wiki/List_of_Argentine_universities "List of Argentine universities") across the country, and 46 private ones.[[357]](#cite_note-378)

In 2010, 7.1% of people over age 20 had graduated from university.[[356]](#cite_note-educ2-377) The public universities of [Buenos Aires](/wiki/University_of_Buenos_Aires "University of Buenos Aires"), [Córdoba](/wiki/Universidad_Nacional_de_C%C3%B3rdoba "Universidad Nacional de Córdoba"), [La Plata](/wiki/Universidad_Nacional_de_La_Plata "Universidad Nacional de La Plata"), [Rosario](/wiki/Universidad_Nacional_de_Rosario "Universidad Nacional de Rosario"), and the [National Technological University](/wiki/National_Technological_University "National Technological University") are some of the most important. The Argentine state guarantees universal, secular and free-of-charge public education for all levels.[[U]](#cite_note-379) Responsibility for educational supervision is organised at the federal and individual provincial states. In the last decades the role of the private sector has grown across all educational stages.

### Health

Main article: [Health care in Argentina](/wiki/Health_care_in_Argentina "Health care in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Nuevo_HECA_Rosario.jpg/250px-Nuevo_HECA_Rosario.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nuevo_HECA_Rosario.jpg)

[Clemente Álvarez Emergency Hospital](/wiki/Hospital_de_Emergencias_Clemente_%C3%81lvarez "Hospital de Emergencias Clemente Álvarez") in [Rosario](/wiki/Rosario,_Santa_Fe "Rosario, Santa Fe")

Healthcare is provided through a combination of employer and labour union-sponsored plans (*Obras Sociales*), government insurance plans, public hospitals and clinics and through private health insurance plans. Healthcare cooperatives number over 300 (of which 200 are related to [labour unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union")) and provide healthcare for half the population; the national INSSJP (popularly known as PAMI) covers nearly all of the five million senior citizens.[[358]](#cite_note-iadb-380)

There are more than 153,000 hospital beds, 121,000 physicians and 37,000 dentists, ratios comparable to [developed nations](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country").[[359]](#cite_note-deis-381)[[360]](#cite_note-undata-382) The relatively high access to medical care has historically resulted in mortality patterns and trends similar to developed nations': from 1953 to 2005, deaths from [cardiovascular disease](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease") increased from 20% to 23% of the total, those from [tumors](/wiki/Tumors "Tumors") from 14% to 20%, [respiratory](/wiki/Respiratory "Respiratory") problems from 7% to 14%, [digestive](/wiki/Digestive_system "Digestive system") maladies (non-infectious) from 7% to 11%, strokes a steady 7%, injuries, 6%, and [infectious](/wiki/Infection "Infection") diseases, 4%. Causes related to [senility](/wiki/Senility "Senility") led to many of the rest. Infant deaths have fallen from 19% of all deaths in 1953 to 3% in 2005.[[359]](#cite_note-deis-381)[[361]](#cite_note-un57-383)

The availability of healthcare has also reduced [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") from 70 per 1000 live births in 1948[[362]](#cite_note-un97-384) to 12.1 in 2009[[359]](#cite_note-deis-381) and raised [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") at birth from 60 years to 76.[[362]](#cite_note-un97-384) Though these figures compare favourably with global averages, they fall short of levels in developed nations and in 2006, Argentina ranked fourth in Latin America.[[360]](#cite_note-undata-382)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Argentina](/wiki/Culture_of_Argentina "Culture of Argentina")

See also: [List of Argentines](/wiki/List_of_Argentines "List of Argentines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Sol_de_mayo_moneda.png/250px-Sol_de_mayo_moneda.png)](/wiki/File:Sol_de_mayo_moneda.png)

The [Sun of May](/wiki/Sun_of_May "Sun of May") on the [first Argentine coin](/wiki/Asamblea_del_A%C3%B1o_XIII "Asamblea del Año XIII"), 1813

Argentina is a [multicultural country](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") with significant European influences. Modern Argentine culture has been largely influenced by [Italian](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_people "Spanish people") and other European immigration from [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Northern_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland"), among others. Its cities are largely characterised by both the prevalence of people of European descent, and of conscious imitation of American and European styles in fashion, architecture and design.[[363]](#cite_note-frommer-385)

Museums, cinemas, and galleries are abundant in all the large urban centres, as well as traditional establishments such as literary bars, or bars offering [live music](/wiki/Live_music "Live music") of a variety of genres although there are lesser elements of [Amerindian](/wiki/Amerindian "Amerindian") and [African](/wiki/African_culture "African culture") influences, particularly in the fields of music and art.[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford200691-386) The other big influence is the [gauchos](/wiki/Gaucho "Gaucho") and their traditional country lifestyle of self-reliance.[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford2006123-387) Indigenous American traditions have been absorbed into the general cultural milieu.
Argentine writer [Ernesto Sabato](/wiki/Ernesto_Sabato "Ernesto Sabato") has reflected on the nature of the culture of Argentina as follows:

> With the primitive Hispanic American reality fractured in La Plata Basin due to immigration, its inhabitants have come to be somewhat dual with all the dangers but also with all the advantages of that condition: because of our European roots, we deeply link the nation with the enduring values of the Old World; because of our condition of Americans we link ourselves to the rest of the continent, through the folklore of the interior and the old Castilian that unifies us, feeling somehow the vocation of the *Patria Grande* San Martín and Bolívar once imagined.

— [Ernesto Sabato](/wiki/Ernesto_Sabato "Ernesto Sabato"), *La cultura en la encrucijada nacional* (1976)[[366]](#cite_note-388)

### Literature

Main article: [Argentine literature](/wiki/Argentine_literature "Argentine literature")

[![Mosaic image showing the four photographs](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Argentine_literature.jpg/250px-Argentine_literature.jpg)](/wiki/File:Argentine_literature.jpg)

Four Argentine writers. Top left to bottom right: [Julio Cortázar](/wiki/Julio_Cort%C3%A1zar "Julio Cortázar"), [Victoria Ocampo](/wiki/Victoria_Ocampo "Victoria Ocampo"), [Jorge Luis Borges](/wiki/Jorge_Luis_Borges "Jorge Luis Borges"), and [Adolfo Bioy Casares](/wiki/Adolfo_Bioy_Casares "Adolfo Bioy Casares").

Although Argentina's rich literary history began around 1550,[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERivas198911-389) it reached full independence with [Esteban Echeverría](/wiki/Esteban_Echeverr%C3%ADa "Esteban Echeverría")'s *El Matadero*, a [romantic](/wiki/Romantic_literature "Romantic literature") landmark that played a significant role in the development of 19th century's Argentine narrative,[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterLockhartLockhart199899-390) split by the ideological divide between the popular, federalist epic of [José Hernández](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Hern%C3%A1ndez_(writer) "José Hernández (writer)")' *[Martín Fierro](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_Fierro "Martín Fierro")* and the elitist and cultured discourse of [Sarmiento](/wiki/Domingo_Faustino_Sarmiento "Domingo Faustino Sarmiento")'s masterpiece, *[Facundo](/wiki/Facundo "Facundo")*.[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterLockhartLockhart199813,_101YoungCisneros201051-391)

The [Modernist](/wiki/Modernist_literature "Modernist literature") movement advanced into the 20th century including exponents such as [Leopoldo Lugones](/wiki/Leopoldo_Lugones "Leopoldo Lugones") and poet [Alfonsina Storni](/wiki/Alfonsina_Storni "Alfonsina Storni");[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungCisneros201051–52-392) it was followed by [Vanguardism](/wiki/Vanguardism "Vanguardism"), with [Ricardo Güiraldes](/wiki/Ricardo_G%C3%BCiraldes "Ricardo Güiraldes")'s *[Don Segundo Sombra](/wiki/Don_Segundo_Sombra "Don Segundo Sombra")* as an important reference.[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterLockhartLockhart1998104,_107–09YoungCisneros2010223-393)

[Jorge Luis Borges](/wiki/Jorge_Luis_Borges "Jorge Luis Borges"), Argentina's most acclaimed writer and one of the foremost figures in the [history of literature](/wiki/History_of_literature "History of literature"),[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloom19942-394) found new ways of looking at the modern world in [metaphor](/wiki/Metaphor "Metaphor") and philosophical debate and his influence has extended to authors all over the globe. Short stories such as *[Ficciones](/wiki/Ficciones "Ficciones")* and *[The Aleph](/wiki/The_Aleph_(short_story_collection) "The Aleph (short story collection)")* are among his most famous works. He was a friend and collaborator of [Adolfo Bioy Casares](/wiki/Adolfo_Bioy_Casares "Adolfo Bioy Casares"), who wrote one of the most praised [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") [novels](/wiki/Novel "Novel"), *[The Invention of Morel](/wiki/The_Invention_of_Morel "The Invention of Morel")*.[[373]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungCisneros201052,_80-395) [Julio Cortázar](/wiki/Julio_Cort%C3%A1zar "Julio Cortázar"), one of the leading members of the [Latin American Boom](/wiki/Latin_American_Boom "Latin American Boom") and a major name in 20th century literature,[[374]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungCisneros201079,_144-396) influenced an entire generation of writers in the Americas and Europe.[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungCisneros20103,_144-397)

A remarkable episode in Argentine literary history is the social and literarial dialectica between the so-called [Florida group](/wiki/Florida_group "Florida group"), named this way because its members used to meet together at [Confitería Richmond](/wiki/Confiter%C3%ADa_Richmond "Confitería Richmond") at Florida street and published in the *[Martín Fierro](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_Fierro_(magazine) "Martín Fierro (magazine)")* magazine, such as [Jorge Luis Borges](/wiki/Jorge_Luis_Borges "Jorge Luis Borges"), [Leopoldo Marechal](/wiki/Leopoldo_Marechal "Leopoldo Marechal"), [Antonio Berni](/wiki/Antonio_Berni "Antonio Berni") (artist), among others; versus the [Boedo group](/wiki/Boedo_group "Boedo group") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grupo_de_Boedo "es:Grupo de Boedo")] of [Roberto Arlt](/wiki/Roberto_Arlt "Roberto Arlt"), [César Tiempo](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Tiempo "César Tiempo"), [Homero Manzi](/wiki/Homero_Manzi "Homero Manzi") (tango composer), that used to meet at the
[Café El Japonés](/w/index.php?title=Caf%C3%A9_El_Japon%C3%A9s&action=edit&redlink=1 "Café El Japonés (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caf%C3%A9_El_Japon%C3%A9s "es:Café El Japonés")] and published their works with the [Editorial Claridad](/w/index.php?title=Editorial_Claridad&action=edit&redlink=1 "Editorial Claridad (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Editorial_Claridad "es:Editorial Claridad")], with both the cafe and the publisher located at Boedo Avenue.

Other highly regarded Argentine writers, poets and [essayists](/wiki/Essay "Essay") include [Estanislao del Campo](/wiki/Estanislao_del_Campo "Estanislao del Campo"), [Eugenio Cambaceres](/wiki/Eugenio_Cambaceres "Eugenio Cambaceres"), [Pedro Bonifacio Palacios](/wiki/Pedro_Bonifacio_Palacios "Pedro Bonifacio Palacios"), [Hugo Wast](/wiki/Hugo_Wast "Hugo Wast"), [Benito Lynch](/wiki/Benito_Lynch "Benito Lynch"), [Enrique Banchs](/wiki/Enrique_Banchs "Enrique Banchs"), [Oliverio Girondo](/wiki/Oliverio_Girondo "Oliverio Girondo"), [Ezequiel Martínez Estrada](/wiki/Ezequiel_Mart%C3%ADnez_Estrada "Ezequiel Martínez Estrada"), [Victoria Ocampo](/wiki/Victoria_Ocampo "Victoria Ocampo"), [Leopoldo Marechal](/wiki/Leopoldo_Marechal "Leopoldo Marechal"), [Silvina Ocampo](/wiki/Silvina_Ocampo "Silvina Ocampo"), [Roberto Arlt](/wiki/Roberto_Arlt "Roberto Arlt"), [Eduardo Mallea](/wiki/Eduardo_Mallea "Eduardo Mallea"), [Manuel Mujica Láinez](/wiki/Manuel_Mujica_L%C3%A1inez "Manuel Mujica Láinez"), [Ernesto Sábato](/wiki/Ernesto_S%C3%A1bato "Ernesto Sábato"), [Silvina Bullrich](/wiki/Silvina_Bullrich "Silvina Bullrich"), [Rodolfo Walsh](/wiki/Rodolfo_Walsh "Rodolfo Walsh"), [María Elena Walsh](/wiki/Mar%C3%ADa_Elena_Walsh "María Elena Walsh"), [Tomás Eloy Martínez](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Eloy_Mart%C3%ADnez "Tomás Eloy Martínez"), [Manuel Puig](/wiki/Manuel_Puig "Manuel Puig"), [Alejandra Pizarnik](/wiki/Alejandra_Pizarnik "Alejandra Pizarnik"), and [Osvaldo Soriano](/wiki/Osvaldo_Soriano "Osvaldo Soriano").[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterLockhartLockhart199866,_85,_97–121McCloskeyBurford200643Díaz_Alejandro197022,_91YoungCisneros201051–54-398)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Argentina](/wiki/Music_of_Argentina "Music of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Mercedes_Sosa%2C_by_Annemarie_Heinrich.jpg/250px-Mercedes_Sosa%2C_by_Annemarie_Heinrich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mercedes_Sosa,_by_Annemarie_Heinrich.jpg)

Photograph of [Mercedes Sosa](/wiki/Mercedes_Sosa "Mercedes Sosa") by [Annemarie Heinrich](/wiki/Annemarie_Heinrich "Annemarie Heinrich")

[Tango](/wiki/Tango "Tango"), a *[Rioplatense](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Río de la Plata")* musical genre with European and African influences,[[377]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller200486-399) is one of Argentina's international cultural symbols.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterLockhartLockhart1998121-400) The golden age of tango, from 1930 to the mid-1950s, mirrored that of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and [swing](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music") in the United States, featuring large orchestras such as those of [Osvaldo Pugliese](/wiki/Osvaldo_Pugliese "Osvaldo Pugliese"), [Aníbal Troilo](/wiki/An%C3%ADbal_Troilo "Aníbal Troilo"), [Francisco Canaro](/wiki/Francisco_Canaro "Francisco Canaro"), [Julio de Caro](/wiki/Julio_de_Caro "Julio de Caro") and [Juan d'Arienzo](/wiki/Juan_d%27Arienzo "Juan d'Arienzo").[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford200643-401) After 1955, virtuoso [Astor Piazzolla](/wiki/Astor_Piazzolla "Astor Piazzolla") popularised *[Nuevo tango](/wiki/Nuevo_tango "Nuevo tango")*, a subtler and more intellectual trend for the genre.[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford200643-401)
Tango enjoys worldwide popularity nowadays with groups such as [Gotan Project](/wiki/Gotan_Project "Gotan Project"), [Bajofondo](/wiki/Bajofondo "Bajofondo") and [Tanghetto](/wiki/Tanghetto "Tanghetto").

Argentina developed strong classical music and dance scenes that gave rise to renowned artists such as [Alberto Ginastera](/wiki/Alberto_Ginastera "Alberto Ginastera"), composer; [Alberto Lysy](/wiki/Alberto_Lysy "Alberto Lysy"), violinist; [Martha Argerich](/wiki/Martha_Argerich "Martha Argerich") and [Eduardo Delgado](/wiki/Eduardo_Delgado "Eduardo Delgado"), pianists; [Daniel Barenboim](/wiki/Daniel_Barenboim "Daniel Barenboim"), pianist and [symphonic orchestra](/wiki/Symphonic_orchestra "Symphonic orchestra") director; [José Cura](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Cura "José Cura") and [Marcelo Álvarez](/wiki/Marcelo_%C3%81lvarez "Marcelo Álvarez"), tenors; and to [ballet dancers](/wiki/Ballet_dancer "Ballet dancer") [Jorge Donn](/wiki/Jorge_Donn "Jorge Donn"), [José Neglia](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Neglia "José Neglia"), [Norma Fontenla](/wiki/Norma_Fontenla "Norma Fontenla"), *Maximiliano Guerra*, [Paloma Herrera](/wiki/Paloma_Herrera "Paloma Herrera"), [Marianela Núñez](/wiki/Marianela_N%C3%BA%C3%B1ez "Marianela Núñez"), [Iñaki Urlezaga](/wiki/I%C3%B1aki_Urlezaga "Iñaki Urlezaga") and [Julio Bocca](/wiki/Julio_Bocca "Julio Bocca").[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford200643-401)

A national Argentine folk style emerged in the 1930s from dozens of regional musical genres and went on to influence the entirety of [Latin American music](/wiki/Latin_American_music "Latin American music"). Some of its interpreters, such as [Atahualpa Yupanqui](/wiki/Atahualpa_Yupanqui "Atahualpa Yupanqui") and [Mercedes Sosa](/wiki/Mercedes_Sosa "Mercedes Sosa"), achieved worldwide acclaim. The [romantic ballad](/wiki/Romantic_ballad "Romantic ballad") genre included singers of international fame such as [Sandro de América](/wiki/Sandro_de_Am%C3%A9rica "Sandro de América"). [Tenor saxophonist](/wiki/Tenor_saxophone "Tenor saxophone") [Leandro "Gato" Barbieri](/wiki/Gato_Barbieri "Gato Barbieri") and composer and [big band](/wiki/Big_band "Big band") conductor [Lalo Schifrin](/wiki/Lalo_Schifrin "Lalo Schifrin") are among the most internationally successful Argentine jazz musicians.

[Argentine rock](/wiki/Argentine_rock "Argentine rock") developed as a distinct musical style in the mid-1960s, when Buenos Aires and Rosario became cradles of aspiring musicians.
Founding bands such as [Los Gatos](/wiki/Los_Gatos_(band) "Los Gatos (band)"), [Sui Generis](/wiki/Sui_Generis "Sui Generis"), [Almendra](/wiki/Almendra_(band) "Almendra (band)") and [Manal](/wiki/Manal "Manal") were followed by [Seru Giran](/wiki/Seru_Giran "Seru Giran"), [Los Abuelos de la Nada](/wiki/Los_Abuelos_de_la_Nada "Los Abuelos de la Nada"), [Soda Stereo](/wiki/Soda_Stereo "Soda Stereo") and [Patricio Rey y sus Redonditos de Ricota](/wiki/Patricio_Rey_y_sus_Redonditos_de_Ricota "Patricio Rey y sus Redonditos de Ricota"), with prominent artists including [Gustavo Cerati](/wiki/Gustavo_Cerati "Gustavo Cerati"), [Litto Nebbia](/wiki/Litto_Nebbia "Litto Nebbia"), [Andrés Calamaro](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Calamaro "Andrés Calamaro"), [Luis Alberto Spinetta](/wiki/Luis_Alberto_Spinetta "Luis Alberto Spinetta"), [Charly García](/wiki/Charly_Garc%C3%ADa "Charly García"), [Fito Páez](/wiki/Fito_P%C3%A1ez "Fito Páez") and [León Gieco](/wiki/Le%C3%B3n_Gieco "León Gieco").[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford200643-401)

A dance and a musical genre popular at present is [Cachengue](/w/index.php?title=Cachengue&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cachengue (page does not exist)"), a subgenre of [Argentine cumbia](/wiki/Argentine_cumbia "Argentine cumbia") and [reggaeton](/wiki/Reggaeton "Reggaeton") spreading in popularity in nearby countries such as [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay"), and [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia").[[380]](#cite_note-402)

### Theatre and cinema

Main articles: [Theatre in Argentina](/wiki/Theatre_in_Argentina "Theatre in Argentina") and [Cinema of Argentina](/wiki/Cinema_of_Argentina "Cinema of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Andy_Muschietti_at_53rd_Saturn_Awards_2026-1470.jpg/250px-Andy_Muschietti_at_53rd_Saturn_Awards_2026-1470.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andy_Muschietti_at_53rd_Saturn_Awards_2026-1470.jpg)

[Andy Muschietti](/wiki/Andy_Muschietti "Andy Muschietti"), director of *[It](/wiki/It_(2017_film) "It (2017 film)")*, the [highest-grossing horror film of all time](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_horror_films "List of highest-grossing horror films").[[381]](#cite_note-403)[[382]](#cite_note-404)

Buenos Aires is one of the great theatre capitals of the world,[[383]](#cite_note-405) with a scene of international caliber centred on [Corrientes Avenue](/wiki/Corrientes_Avenue "Corrientes Avenue"), "the street that never sleeps", sometimes referred to as the [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)") of Buenos Aires.[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterLockhartLockhart199848-406) [Teatro Colón](/wiki/Teatro_Col%C3%B3n "Teatro Colón") is a global landmark for [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera") and classical performances; its acoustics are considered among the world's top five.[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong200921–25-407)[[V]](#cite_note-408)

The Argentine film industry has historically been one of the three most developed in [Latin American cinema](/wiki/Latin_American_cinema "Latin American cinema"), along with those produced in [Mexico](/wiki/Cinema_of_Mexico "Cinema of Mexico") and [Brazil](/wiki/Cinema_of_Brazil "Cinema of Brazil").[[386]](#cite_note-409)[[387]](#cite_note-410) It started in 1896. By the early 1930s, it was Latin America's leading film producer, a place it kept until the early 1950s.[[388]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKing200036-411) The world's first [animated feature films](/wiki/List_of_animated_feature_films "List of animated feature films") were made and released in Argentina, by cartoonist [Quirino Cristiani](/wiki/Quirino_Cristiani "Quirino Cristiani"), in 1917 and 1918.[[389]](#cite_note-412)

Argentine films have achieved worldwide recognition: Argentina has won two [Academy Awards for Best Foreign Language Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film"), for *[The Official Story](/wiki/The_Official_Story "The Official Story")* (1985) and *[The Secret in Their Eyes](/wiki/The_Secret_in_Their_Eyes "The Secret in Their Eyes")* (2009). Argentine composers [Luis Enrique Bacalov](/wiki/Luis_Enrique_Bacalov "Luis Enrique Bacalov") and [Gustavo Santaolalla](/wiki/Gustavo_Santaolalla "Gustavo Santaolalla") have been honoured with [Academy Awards for Best Original Score](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Score "Academy Award for Best Original Score"), and [Armando Bó](/wiki/Armando_B%C3%B3_(screenwriter) "Armando Bó (screenwriter)") and [Nicolás Giacobone](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Giacobone "Nicolás Giacobone") shared in the [Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay") for 2014.

The [Argentine French](/wiki/French_Argentine "French Argentine") actress [Bérénice Bejo](/wiki/B%C3%A9r%C3%A9nice_Bejo "Bérénice Bejo") received a nomination for the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actress "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress") in 2011 and won the [César Award for Best Actress](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Award_for_Best_Actress "César Award for Best Actress") and won the [Best Actress](/wiki/Best_Actress_Award_(Cannes_Film_Festival) "Best Actress Award (Cannes Film Festival)") award in the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival") for her role in the film *[The Past](/wiki/The_Past_(2013_film) "The Past (2013 film)")*.[[390]](#cite_note-CannesAwards-413) Argentina has won seventeen [Goya Awards for Best Spanish Language Foreign Film](/wiki/Goya_Award_for_Best_Spanish_Language_Foreign_Film "Goya Award for Best Spanish Language Foreign Film"), being by far the most awarded country in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") with twenty-four nominations. Many other Argentine films have been acclaimed by international critique. In 2013, about 100 full-length motion pictures were being created annually.[[391]](#cite_note-414)

### Visual arts and architecture

See also: [Argentine painting](/wiki/Argentine_painting "Argentine painting") and [Architecture of Argentina](/wiki/Architecture_of_Argentina "Architecture of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Fuente_de_las_Nereidas.jpg/250px-Fuente_de_las_Nereidas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fuente_de_las_Nereidas.jpg)

*Las Nereidas Font* by [Lola Mora](/wiki/Lola_Mora "Lola Mora")

Some of the best-known Argentine painters are [Cándido López](/wiki/C%C3%A1ndido_L%C3%B3pez "Cándido López") and [Florencio Molina Campos](/wiki/Florencio_Molina_Campos "Florencio Molina Campos") ([Naïve style](/wiki/Na%C3%AFve_art "Naïve art")); [Ernesto de la Cárcova](/wiki/Ernesto_de_la_C%C3%A1rcova "Ernesto de la Cárcova") and [Eduardo Sívori](/wiki/Eduardo_S%C3%ADvori "Eduardo Sívori") ([Realism](/wiki/Realism_(art) "Realism (art)")); [Fernando Fader](/wiki/Fernando_Fader "Fernando Fader") ([Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism")); [Pío Collivadino](/wiki/P%C3%ADo_Collivadino "Pío Collivadino"), [Atilio Malinverno](/wiki/Atilio_Malinverno "Atilio Malinverno") and [Cesáreo Bernaldo de Quirós](/wiki/Ces%C3%A1reo_Bernaldo_de_Quir%C3%B3s "Cesáreo Bernaldo de Quirós") ([Postimpressionism](/wiki/Postimpressionism "Postimpressionism")); [Emilio Pettoruti](/wiki/Emilio_Pettoruti "Emilio Pettoruti") ([Cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism")); [Julio Barragán](/wiki/Julio_Barrag%C3%A1n "Julio Barragán") ([Concretism](/wiki/Concretism_(art) "Concretism (art)") and Cubism) [Antonio Berni](/wiki/Antonio_Berni "Antonio Berni") ([Neofigurativism](/wiki/Neofigurativism "Neofigurativism")); [Roberto Aizenberg](/wiki/Roberto_Aizenberg "Roberto Aizenberg") and [Xul Solar](/wiki/Xul_Solar "Xul Solar") ([Surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism")); [Gyula Košice](/wiki/Gyula_Ko%C5%A1ice "Gyula Košice") ([Constructivism](/wiki/Constructivism_(art) "Constructivism (art)")); [Eduardo Mac Entyre](/wiki/Eduardo_Mac_Entyre "Eduardo Mac Entyre") ([Generative art](/wiki/Generative_art "Generative art")); [Luis Seoane](/wiki/Luis_Seoane "Luis Seoane"), *Carlos Torrallardona*, *Luis Aquino*, *Alfredo Gramajo Gutiérrez* ([Modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism")); [Lucio Fontana](/wiki/Lucio_Fontana "Lucio Fontana") ([Spatialism](/wiki/Spatialism "Spatialism")); [Tomás Maldonado](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Maldonado "Tomás Maldonado"), [Guillermo Kuitca](/wiki/Guillermo_Kuitca "Guillermo Kuitca") ([Abstract art](/wiki/Abstract_art "Abstract art")); [León Ferrari](/wiki/Le%C3%B3n_Ferrari "León Ferrari"), [Marta Minujín](/wiki/Marta_Minuj%C3%ADn "Marta Minujín") ([Conceptual art](/wiki/Conceptual_art "Conceptual art")); [Gustavo Cabral](/wiki/Ciruelo_Cabral "Ciruelo Cabral") ([Fantasy art](/wiki/Fantasy_art "Fantasy art")), and [Fabián Pérez (Neoemotionalism)](/wiki/Fabian_Perez "Fabian Perez").

In 1946, Gyula Košice and others created The [Madí Movement](/wiki/Mad%C3%AD_Movement "Madí Movement") in Argentina, which then spread to Europe and the United States, where it had a significant impact.[[392]](#cite_note-415) Tomás Maldonado was one of the main theorists of the [Ulm Model](/wiki/Ulm_School_of_Design "Ulm School of Design") of design education, still highly influential globally. Other Argentine artists of worldwide fame include [Adolfo Bellocq](/wiki/Adolfo_Bellocq "Adolfo Bellocq"), whose [lithographs](/wiki/Lithograph "Lithograph") have been influential since the 1920s, and [Benito Quinquela Martín](/wiki/Benito_Quinquela_Mart%C3%ADn "Benito Quinquela Martín"), the quintessential port painter, inspired by the immigrant-bound [La Boca](/wiki/La_Boca "La Boca") neighbourhood. Internationally laureate sculptors [Erminio Blotta](/wiki/Erminio_Blotta "Erminio Blotta"), [Lola Mora](/wiki/Lola_Mora "Lola Mora") and [Rogelio Yrurtia](/wiki/Rogelio_Yrurtia "Rogelio Yrurtia") authored many of the classical evocative monuments of the Argentine cityscape.[[393]](#cite_note-416)[[394]](#cite_note-417)

The colonisation brought the [Spanish Baroque architecture](/wiki/Spanish_Baroque_architecture "Spanish Baroque architecture"), which can still be appreciated in its simpler *Rioplatense* style in the [reduction](/wiki/Indian_Reductions "Indian Reductions") of [San Ignacio Miní](/wiki/San_Ignacio_Min%C3%AD "San Ignacio Miní"), the [Cathedral of Córdoba](/wiki/Cathedral_of_C%C3%B3rdoba_(Argentina) "Cathedral of Córdoba (Argentina)"), and the Cabildo of Luján. Italian and French influences increased at the beginning of the 19th century with strong [eclectic](/wiki/Eclectic_architecture "Eclectic architecture") overtones that gave the local architecture a unique feeling.[[395]](#cite_note-418)

### Mass media

Main article: [Communications in Argentina](/wiki/Communications_in_Argentina "Communications in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Canal_7_Argentina.JPG/250px-Canal_7_Argentina.JPG)](/wiki/File:Canal_7_Argentina.JPG)

Headquarters of the [Channel 7](/wiki/TV_P%C3%BAblica_Digital_(Argentina) "TV Pública Digital (Argentina)"), the first television station in the country

The print media industry is highly developed in Argentina, with more than two hundred newspapers. The major national ones include *[Clarín](/wiki/Clar%C3%ADn_(Argentine_newspaper) "Clarín (Argentine newspaper)")* (centrist, Latin America's best-seller and the second most widely circulated in the Spanish-speaking world), *[La Nación](/wiki/La_Naci%C3%B3n_(Buenos_Aires) "La Nación (Buenos Aires)")* (centre-right), *[Página/12](/wiki/P%C3%A1gina/12 "Página/12")* (leftist), *[La Voz del Interior](/wiki/La_Voz_del_Interior "La Voz del Interior")* (centre).[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAeberhardBensonPhillips200045-419)[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkstinat201320-420)

Argentina began [the world's first regular radio broadcasting](/wiki/History_of_radio "History of radio") on 27 August 1920, when [Richard Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner")'s *[Parsifal](/wiki/Parsifal "Parsifal")* was aired by a team of medical students led by [Enrique Telémaco Susini](/wiki/Enrique_Tel%C3%A9maco_Susini "Enrique Telémaco Susini") in Buenos Aires' [Teatro Coliseo](/wiki/Teatro_Coliseo "Teatro Coliseo").[[398]](#cite_note-421) In 2002, there were 260 [AM](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting") and 1150 [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") registered radio stations in Argentina.[[399]](#cite_note-422)

The [Argentine television](/wiki/Television_in_Argentina "Television in Argentina") industry is large, diverse and popular across Latin America, with many productions and [TV formats](/wiki/TV_format "TV format") having been exported abroad. Since 1999 Argentines have enjoyed the highest availability of cable and satellite television in Latin America.[[400]](#cite_note-423) In 2014, 87.4% of Argentina's households had access, a rate similar to those in the United States, Canada and Europe.[[401]](#cite_note-424)

As of early 2024, internet penetration in Argentina stood at 88.4% of the total population, and the ratio of cellular mobile connections to the total population was 135.3% (62.14 million active connections).[[402]](#cite_note-425)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Argentine cuisine](/wiki/Argentine_cuisine "Argentine cuisine")

[![Table with a cut of Argentine beef, wine, sauces and spices](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Asado_2005.jpg/250px-Asado_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Asado_2005.jpg)

[Argentine beef](/wiki/Argentine_beef "Argentine beef") as *[asado](/wiki/Asado "Asado")*

Besides many of the pasta, sausage and dessert dishes common to continental Europe, Argentines enjoy a wide variety of Indigenous and [Criollo](/wiki/Criollo_people "Criollo people") creations, including *[empanadas](/wiki/Empanada "Empanada")* (a small stuffed pastry), *[locro](/wiki/Locro "Locro")* (a mixture of corn, beans, meat, bacon, onion, and gourd), *[humita](/wiki/Humita "Humita")* and *[mate](/wiki/Mat%C3%A9 "Maté")*.[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford200679,_199,_221-426)

The country has the highest consumption of [red meat](/wiki/Red_meat "Red meat") in the world,[[404]](#cite_note-427) traditionally prepared as *[asado](/wiki/Asado "Asado")*, the Argentine barbecue. It is made with various types of meats, often including *[chorizo](/wiki/Chorizo "Chorizo")*, [sweetbread](/wiki/Sweetbread "Sweetbread"), [chitterlings](/wiki/Chitterlings "Chitterlings"), and [blood sausage](/wiki/Blood_sausage "Blood sausage").[[405]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford200679-428)

Common desserts include *[facturas](/wiki/Facturas "Facturas")* ([Viennese-style](/wiki/Viennese_cuisine "Viennese cuisine") pastry), [cakes](/wiki/Cake "Cake") and [pancakes](/wiki/Pancake "Pancake") filled with *[dulce de leche](/wiki/Dulce_de_leche "Dulce de leche")* (a sort of milk [caramel](/wiki/Caramel "Caramel") jam), *[alfajores](/wiki/Alfajor "Alfajor")* (shortbread cookies sandwiched together with chocolate, *dulce de leche* or a fruit paste), and *[tortas fritas](/wiki/Torta_frita "Torta frita")* (fried cakes)[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAeberhardBensonPhillips200031McCloskeyBurford200680,_143-429)

[Argentine wine](/wiki/Argentine_wine "Argentine wine"), one of the world's finest,[[407]](#cite_note-430) is an integral part of the local menu. [Malbec](/wiki/Malbec "Malbec"), [Torrontés](/wiki/Torront%C3%A9s "Torrontés"), [Cabernet Sauvignon](/wiki/Cabernet_Sauvignon "Cabernet Sauvignon"), [Syrah](/wiki/Syrah "Syrah") and [Chardonnay](/wiki/Chardonnay "Chardonnay") are some of the most sought-after [varieties](/wiki/International_varieties "International varieties").[[408]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCloskeyBurford2006230,_252,_261–62,_265-431)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Argentina](/wiki/Sport_in_Argentina "Sport in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Lionel_Messi_WC2022.jpg/250px-Lionel_Messi_WC2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lionel_Messi_WC2022.jpg)

Footballer [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi"), eight-time [Ballon d'Or](/wiki/Ballon_d%27Or "Ballon d'Or") winner, is the current captain of the [Argentina national football team](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team").

*[Pato](/wiki/Pato "Pato")* is the [national sport](/wiki/National_sport "National sport"),[[409]](#cite_note-pato1-432) an ancient horseback game locally originated in the early 1600s and predecessor of [horseball](/wiki/Horseball "Horseball").[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaurightParrish2012124–25-433)[[411]](#cite_note-pato2-434)

The most popular sport is [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). Along with [Brazil](/wiki/Brazilian_national_football_team "Brazilian national football team"), [Germany](/wiki/German_national_football_team "German national football team") and [France](/wiki/French_national_football_team "French national football team"), the [men's national team](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") is the only one to have won each of the [World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") (in [1978](/wiki/1978_FIFA_World_Cup "1978 FIFA World Cup"), [1986](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup") and [2022](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup")), [Confederations Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Confederations_Cup "FIFA Confederations Cup"), and the [Olympic gold](/wiki/Football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Football at the Summer Olympics"). They have also won 16 [Copas América](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "Copa América"), 7 [Pan American Gold Medals](/wiki/Football_at_the_Pan_American_Games "Football at the Pan American Games") and many other trophies.[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaurightParrish201214–23-435) [Alfredo Di Stéfano](/wiki/Alfredo_Di_St%C3%A9fano "Alfredo Di Stéfano"), [Diego Maradona](/wiki/Diego_Maradona "Diego Maradona") and [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") are widely considered to be among the best players in the game's history.[[413]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedman200756,_127-436)

The country's [women's field hockey team *Las Leonas*](/wiki/Argentina_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Argentina women's national field hockey team"), is one of the world's most successful with four [Olympic medals](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the Summer Olympics"), two [World Cups](/wiki/Women%27s_Hockey_World_Cup "Women's Hockey World Cup"), a [World League](/wiki/FIH_Hockey_World_League "FIH Hockey World League") and seven [Champions Trophy](/wiki/Hockey_Champions_Trophy "Hockey Champions Trophy").[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaurightParrish201211-437) [Luciana Aymar](/wiki/Luciana_Aymar "Luciana Aymar") is recognised as the best female player in the history of the sport,[[415]](#cite_note-hwc1-438) being the only player to have received the [FIH Player of the Year Award](/wiki/FIH_Player_of_the_Year_Awards "FIH Player of the Year Awards") eight times.[[416]](#cite_note-fih1-439)

[Basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") is a very popular sport. The [men's national team](/wiki/Argentina_national_basketball_team "Argentina national basketball team") is the only one in the [FIBA Americas](/wiki/FIBA_Americas "FIBA Americas") zone that has won the quintuplet crown: [World Championship](/wiki/FIBA_World_Championship "FIBA World Championship"), [Olympic Gold Medal](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the Summer Olympics"), [Diamond Ball](/wiki/FIBA_Diamond_Ball "FIBA Diamond Ball"), [Americas Championship](/wiki/FIBA_Americas_Championship "FIBA Americas Championship"), and [Pan American Gold Medal](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_Pan_American_Games "Basketball at the Pan American Games"). It has also conquered 13 [South American Championships](/wiki/South_American_Basketball_Championship "South American Basketball Championship"), and many other tournaments.[[417]](#cite_note-fiba1-440) [Emanuel Ginóbili](/wiki/Emanuel_Gin%C3%B3bili "Emanuel Ginóbili"), [Luis Scola](/wiki/Luis_Scola "Luis Scola"), [Andrés Nocioni](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Nocioni "Andrés Nocioni"), [Fabricio Oberto](/wiki/Fabricio_Oberto "Fabricio Oberto"), [Pablo Prigioni](/wiki/Pablo_Prigioni "Pablo Prigioni"), [Carlos Delfino](/wiki/Carlos_Delfino "Carlos Delfino") and [Juan Ignacio Sánchez](/wiki/Juan_Ignacio_S%C3%A1nchez "Juan Ignacio Sánchez") are a few of the country's most acclaimed players, all of them part of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association").[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaurightParrish201211-437) Argentina hosted the [Basketball World Cup](/wiki/Basketball_World_Cup "Basketball World Cup") in 1950 and 1990.

[Rugby](/wiki/Rugby_Union "Rugby Union") is another popular sport in Argentina. As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Argentina&action=edit), the [men's national team](/wiki/Argentina_national_rugby_union_team "Argentina national rugby union team"), known as 'Los Pumas' has competed at the [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup") each time it has been held, achieving their highest-ever result in [2007](/wiki/2007_Rugby_World_Cup "2007 Rugby World Cup") when they came third. Since [2012](/wiki/2012_Rugby_Championship "2012 Rugby Championship"), the Los Pumas have competed against [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") & [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa_national_rugby_union_team "South Africa national rugby union team") in [The Rugby Championship](/wiki/The_Rugby_Championship "The Rugby Championship"), the premier international Rugby competition in the Southern Hemisphere. Since 2009 the [secondary men's national team](/wiki/Argentina_Jaguars "Argentina Jaguars") known as the 'Jaguares' has competed against the [US](/wiki/USA_Selects "USA Selects"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada_A_national_rugby_union_team "Canada A national rugby union team"), and [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_rugby_union_team "Uruguay national rugby union team") first teams in the [Americas Rugby Championship](/wiki/Americas_Rugby_Championship "Americas Rugby Championship"), which Los Jaguares have won six out of eight times it has taken place.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Campeonato_Argentino_de_Polo_2010_-_5236515585_2b8cb412de_o.jpg/250px-Campeonato_Argentino_de_Polo_2010_-_5236515585_2b8cb412de_o.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campeonato_Argentino_de_Polo_2010_-_5236515585_2b8cb412de_o.jpg)

[Argentine Polo Open Championship](/wiki/Campeonato_Argentino_Abierto_de_Polo "Campeonato Argentino Abierto de Polo")

Argentina has produced some of the most formidable champions for [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), including [Carlos Monzón](/wiki/Carlos_Monz%C3%B3n "Carlos Monzón"), the best [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") in history;[[418]](#cite_note-thering1-441) [Pascual Pérez](/wiki/Pascual_P%C3%A9rez_(boxer) "Pascual Pérez (boxer)"), one of the most decorated [flyweight](/wiki/Flyweight "Flyweight") boxers of all times; [Horacio Accavallo](/wiki/Horacio_Accavallo "Horacio Accavallo"), the former [WBA](/wiki/World_Boxing_Association "World Boxing Association") and [WBC](/wiki/World_Boxing_Council "World Boxing Council") world flyweight champion; [Víctor Galíndez](/wiki/V%C3%ADctor_Gal%C3%ADndez "Víctor Galíndez"), as of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Argentina&action=edit), record holder for consecutive world [light heavyweight](/wiki/Light_heavyweight "Light heavyweight") title defences and [Nicolino Locche](/wiki/Nicolino_Locche "Nicolino Locche"), nicknamed "The Untouchable" for his masterful defence; they are all inductees into the [International Boxing Hall of Fame](/wiki/International_Boxing_Hall_of_Fame "International Boxing Hall of Fame").[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodríguez2009164–65-442)

[Tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") has been quite popular among people of all ages. [Guillermo Vilas](/wiki/Guillermo_Vilas "Guillermo Vilas") is the greatest Latin American player of the [Open Era](/wiki/History_of_tennis "History of tennis"),[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaurightParrish2012144-443) while [Gabriela Sabatini](/wiki/Gabriela_Sabatini "Gabriela Sabatini") is the most accomplished Argentine female player of all time—having reached number 3 in the [WTA ranking](/wiki/WTA_ranking "WTA ranking"),[[421]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaurightParrish2012135-444) are both inductees into the [International Tennis Hall of Fame](/wiki/International_Tennis_Hall_of_Fame "International Tennis Hall of Fame").[[422]](#cite_note-ithf1-445) Argentina has won the [World Team Cup](/wiki/World_Team_Cup "World Team Cup") four times, in 1980, 2002, [2007](/wiki/2007_ARAG_World_Team_Cup "2007 ARAG World Team Cup") and [2010](/wiki/2010_ARAG_World_Team_Cup "2010 ARAG World Team Cup") and has reached the semifinals of the [Davis Cup](/wiki/Davis_Cup "Davis Cup") 7 times in the last 10 years, losing the finals against Russia in [2006](/wiki/2006_Davis_Cup "2006 Davis Cup") and Spain in [2008](/wiki/2008_Davis_Cup "2008 Davis Cup") and [2011](/wiki/2011_Davis_Cup "2011 Davis Cup"); the Argentine team also played the final in [1981](/wiki/1981_Davis_Cup "1981 Davis Cup"), where they lost against the United States. The national squad won the [2016 Davis Cup](/wiki/2016_Davis_Cup "2016 Davis Cup").

Argentina reigns undisputed in [polo](/wiki/Polo "Polo"), having won more international championships than any other country and been seldom beaten since the 1930s.[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAeberhardBensonPhillips200050–51-446) The [Argentine Polo Championship](/wiki/Campeonato_Argentino_Abierto_de_Polo "Campeonato Argentino Abierto de Polo") is the sport's most important international team trophy. The country is home to most of the world's top players, among them [Adolfo Cambiaso](/wiki/Adolfo_Cambiaso "Adolfo Cambiaso"), the best in Polo history.[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaurightParrish2012128-447)

Historically, Argentina has had a strong showing within [auto racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing"). [Juan Manuel Fangio](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_Fangio "Juan Manuel Fangio") was a five-time [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") world champion under four different teams, winning 102 of his 184 international races, and is widely ranked as the greatest driver of all time.[[425]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaurightParrish201298Dougall2013170–171-448) Other distinguished racers were [Oscar Alfredo Gálvez](/wiki/Oscar_Alfredo_G%C3%A1lvez "Oscar Alfredo Gálvez"), [Juan Gálvez](/wiki/Juan_G%C3%A1lvez_(racing_driver) "Juan Gálvez (racing driver)"), [José Froilán González](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Froil%C3%A1n_Gonz%C3%A1lez "José Froilán González") and [Carlos Reutemann](/wiki/Carlos_Reutemann "Carlos Reutemann").[[426]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArbena1999147Dougall2013170–171,_195-449)
